# APT10 — APT Profile

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | China (MSS Tianjin State Security Bureau) |
| Aliases | menuPass, Stone Panda, Red Apollo, Cicada, POTASSIUM, BRONZE RIVERSIDE, Cloud Hopper |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G0045 |
| First seen | 2006 |
| Status | Active |
| Motivation | Espionage / IP theft |

## Overview
APT10 (MITRE **G0045**, also "menuPass") is a long-running Chinese state-sponsored cyber-espionage group active since at least 2006. It is most infamous for **Operation Cloud Hopper**, a global supply-chain campaign that compromised managed service providers (MSPs) to pivot into their downstream clients. APT10 specialises in stealing intellectual property and sensitive government/commercial data, with a historic emphasis on Japanese organisations and broad collection across aerospace, defence, healthcare, and technology sectors worldwide.

## Attribution & Origin
APT10 is attributed to the **PRC Ministry of State Security, Tianjin State Security Bureau**. In December 2018 the US Department of Justice indicted **Zhu Hua** and **Zhang Shilong**, alleging they acted in association with the Tianjin SSB and operated through front company **Huaying Haitai Science and Technology Development Company**. The indictment, coordinated with allied attributions (UK NCSC, others), gives high-confidence attribution for the core historic intrusion set. Activity continues to be tracked under newer vendor clusters (Cicada/Cyber Tempest, BRONZE RIVERSIDE, Purple Typhoon).

## Motivation & Objectives
State-directed espionage and intellectual-property theft to advance PRC economic, military, and technological objectives. Cloud Hopper demonstrated a strategic preference for **scale via trusted relationships** — compromising one MSP to reach many victims — minimising direct targeting footprint while maximising collection.

## Target Profile
Global footprint with a documented emphasis on **Japanese** organisations, and victims across the US, UK, Europe, India, and the **APAC region including Australia**. Sectors include MSPs/IT service providers, government, defence, aerospace, healthcare, maritime, biotechnology, energy, finance, and manufacturing.

**APAC / Australia note:** Operation Cloud Hopper explicitly affected MSP clients across **at least a dozen countries**, and Australian organisations were impacted as downstream MSP customers — a key reason the ACSC and allied agencies issued coordinated warnings on the MSP supply-chain threat. The MSP-pivot model makes Australian enterprises relying on shared/offshore IT providers a high-value indirect target. Japan remains the single most heavily targeted nation in the APAC theatre.

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK
**Initial Access**
- T1566.001 Spearphishing Attachment — well-researched lures delivering ChChes/PlugX/RedLeaves.
- T1199 Trusted Relationship — core Cloud Hopper TTP: compromise an MSP to reach its clients.
- T1078 Valid Accounts — stolen MSP/admin credentials reused into client networks.

**Persistence & Privilege**
- T1053.005 Scheduled Task/Job.
- T1574.002 DLL Side-Loading — signed binaries (incl. abused Windows Defender/legit EXEs) load malicious DLLs.

**Credential Access**
- T1003.001 LSASS Memory dumping (Mimikatz, PwDump6).

**Lateral Movement**
- T1021.002 SMB/Windows Admin Shares.

**Defense Evasion**
- T1027 Obfuscated Files/Information.
- T1090 Proxy / connection proxying through compromised infrastructure.

**Command & Control**
- T1071.001 Web Protocols (HTTP/HTTPS).
- T1105 Ingress Tool Transfer.

**Exfiltration**
- T1567.002 Exfiltration to Cloud Storage; data staged and routed via MSP/CSP infrastructure.

## Tooling & Malware
- **PlugX** — primary persistent RAT (side-loaded).
- **RedLeaves** — custom full-featured backdoor.
- **QuasarRAT** — open-source RAT, modified/recompiled.
- **ChChes** — early-stage downloader/backdoor.
- **Anel / UPPERCUT** — backdoors used in later A41APT campaign.
- **SodaMaster** — fileless backdoor (A41APT).
- **Mimikatz / PwDump6** — credential theft.

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **2006–2009** — Earliest assessed menuPass activity.
- **2016** — Shift to PlugX, ChChes, Quasar, RedLeaves toolset.
- **2017** — **Operation Cloud Hopper** exposed (PwC UK / BAE Systems): global MSP supply-chain compromise.
- **Dec 2018** — US DOJ indicts Zhu Hua and Zhang Shilong (Tianjin SSB / Huaying Haitai).
- **2019–2020** — Cicada campaigns vs Japan-linked organisations worldwide (Symantec).
- **2020–2021** — **A41APT** campaign: SodaMaster, Anel, fileless techniques (documented by Kaspersky/JPCERT).
- **2022–2026** — Continued activity under BRONZE RIVERSIDE / Purple Typhoon clustering.

## Detection & Hunting
- Monitor MSP/remote-management connections: anomalous logins from MSP service accounts, off-hours access, new admin accounts.
- Hunt DLL side-loading and abuse of signed system binaries loading unexpected DLLs.
- LSASS access/dumping alerts (Mimikatz/PwDump patterns).
- Scheduled-task creation tied to unknown binaries.
- Cloud-storage exfiltration anomalies and proxying through atypical infrastructure.
- Threat-hunt for PlugX/RedLeaves/QuasarRAT/SodaMaster IOCs and C2 beacons.

## Mitigations
- Harden and segment MSP/third-party remote access; enforce least privilege and dedicated PAM for service accounts.
- Phishing-resistant MFA on all MSP and administrative access (T1199/T1078 mitigation).
- Application allow-listing/WDAC to block DLL side-loading.
- Credential-guard / LSASS protection; restrict and monitor admin tooling.
- Network segmentation and egress monitoring to limit lateral movement and exfiltration.
- Continuous MSP supply-chain risk assessment and contractual security requirements.

## Activity Log
<!-- Append-only -->
- _(seed)_ 2026-06-17 — Profile created.

## Trend Tracking
| Date | Event Type | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| | | | |

## References
- https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0045/
- https://malpedia.caad.fkie.fraunhofer.de/actor/apt10
- https://www.pwc.co.uk/cyber-security/pdf/pwc-uk-operation-cloud-hopper-report-april-2017.pdf
- https://www.ncsc.gov.uk/files/APT10%20advisory%20v2.pdf
- https://www.picussecurity.com/resource/blog/a41apt-of-the-apt10
- https://www.huntress.com/threat-library/threat-actors/apt10
- https://www.cfr.org/cyber-operations/apt-10

# APT28 — APT Profile

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | Russia — GRU Unit 26165 (85th GTsSS) |
| Aliases | Fancy Bear, Sofacy, Sednit, Forest Blizzard (ex-STRONTIUM), Pawn Storm, BlueDelta |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G0007 |
| First seen | 2004 |
| Status | Active |
| Motivation | Espionage, information operations / influence |

## Overview
APT28 is a Russian state-sponsored cyber-espionage group operating since at least 2004 under the GRU's 85th Main Special Service Center (Military Unit 26165). It is one of the most prolific and well-documented nation-state actors, responsible for high-profile hack-and-leak operations, election interference, anti-doping retaliation, and sustained intelligence collection against governments, militaries, and the defense-industrial base.

APT28 blends classic spear-phishing and credential-theft tradecraft with advanced capabilities including the first in-the-wild UEFI rootkit (LoJax), Linux malware (Drovorub), and close-access Wi-Fi operations (the 2024 "Nearest Neighbor" attack). Since Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, the group has heavily targeted Western logistics, transportation, and technology entities involved in coordinating aid to Ukraine.

The group is not financially motivated; its objectives are strategic intelligence collection and influence operations in support of Russian government and military goals. It has been the subject of multiple US DOJ indictments and EU/UK sanctions.

## Attribution & Origin
- **Country / sponsor:** Russia — GRU (Main Intelligence Directorate), 85th Main Special Service Center (GTsSS), Military Unit 26165.
- **July 13, 2018:** DOJ/Special Counsel indictment of 12 GRU officers (Units 26165 and 74455) for the 2016 DNC/DCCC/Clinton-campaign hack-and-leak.
- **October 4, 2018:** DOJ indictment of 7 GRU (Unit 26165) officers for close-access and remote operations (2014–2018) against WADA, USADA, OPCW, the Spiez lab, Westinghouse, and FIFA/anti-doping bodies.
- **October 22, 2020:** EU Council sanctioned Dmitry Badin, Igor Kostyukov, and the GRU 85th GTsSS entity over the 2015 Bundestag hack (travel bans, asset freezes).
- **2020:** UK sanctions on two GRU officers and Unit 26165 for the Bundestag attack.
- **July 18, 2025:** NCSC/FCDO attributed the "Authentic Antics" malware to APT28/Unit 26165 and sanctioned GRU Units 26165, 29155, and 74455 plus 18 GRU officers/agents.

## Motivation & Objectives
State-directed cyber-espionage and information operations in support of Russian geopolitical objectives: intelligence collection on governments, militaries, defense, and foreign policy; influence and hack-and-leak operations (election interference, anti-doping retaliation); and, since 2022, collection against entities supporting Ukraine (logistics, defense aid, technology). Not financially motivated.

## Target Profile
**Sectors:** Government, military/defense, foreign-affairs ministries, defense-industrial base, energy, aerospace, think tanks/NGOs, media/journalists, political parties/campaigns, anti-doping and international sporting/chemical-weapons bodies, education, and (since 2022) logistics/transportation (air, sea, rail) and technology/IT services.

**Regions:** Primarily NATO/EU members, Ukraine, the US, and the Caucasus (Georgia); also Middle East and South America.

**APAC / Australia targeting:** Yes. ASD's ACSC co-authored and co-sealed the May 21, 2025 joint advisory *"Russian GRU Targeting Western Logistics Entities and Technology Companies"* (CISA AA25-141A) alongside CISA, NSA, FBI, and 15+ allied agencies. ACSC published its own version warning Australian logistics and tech firms involved in Ukraine-aid coordination, stating "ASD expects similar targeting and TTP use to continue." Russian state cyber activity is also covered in ASD's Annual Cyber Threat Report 2024–25. The APAC/Australia exposure is principally supply-chain and Ukraine-aid logistics rather than a standalone Australia campaign.

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK

### Reconnaissance / Resource Development
- **T1598.003 – Spearphishing Link (for info):** Credential-phishing emails with links to spoofed login/credential-harvesting pages.
- **T1588.002 – Obtain Capabilities: Tool:** Adopts open-source tooling (Koadic, Mimikatz, Responder).

### Initial Access
- **T1566.001 – Spearphishing Attachment:** Malicious Office and RAR attachments delivering loaders/backdoors.
- **T1133 – External Remote Services:** Uses Tor and commercial VPNs to route brute-force authentication against externally-facing services.
- **T1190 – Exploit Public-Facing Application:** Exploits internet-facing systems (webmail/Exchange).

### Execution
- **T1059.003 – Windows Command Shell:** Loader Trojans use cmd.exe/batch scripts (e.g., the Nearest Neighbor campaign C0051).
- **T1559.002 – Inter-Process Communication: DDE:** Delivered JHUHUGIT and Koadic via PowerShell launched through DDE in Word.
- **T1203 – Exploitation for Client Execution:** Weaponized Office/Outlook vulns (CVE-2023-23397, CVE-2017-0262/0263).

### Persistence
- **T1546.015 – Event Triggered Execution: COM Hijacking:** Replaces the legitimate MMDeviceEnumerator COM object with its payload.
- **T1098 – Account Manipulation:** Modifies Exchange mailbox permissions to maintain mail access (2022–25 logistics campaign).

### Privilege Escalation
- **T1068 – Exploitation for Privilege Escalation:** Uses GooseEgg to abuse CVE-2022-38028 (Print Spooler) to run code as SYSTEM.

### Defense Evasion
- **T1218.011 – Rundll32:** Runs CHOPSTICK/loaders via rundll32.exe (e.g., twain_64.dll).
- **T1542.003 – Pre-OS Boot: Bootkit:** Deployed a bootkit with Downdelph and the LoJax UEFI rootkit for firmware-level persistence.

### Credential Access
- **T1003.001 – OS Credential Dumping: LSASS Memory:** Deploys Mimikatz and dumps LSASS via MiniDump.
- **T1110 – Brute Force / Password Spraying:** Large-scale password-spray/brute-force (NSA "Global Brute Force"; reconstituted in the logistics campaign).
- **T1040 – Network Sniffing:** Responder for NBT-NS poisoning to capture hashes; close-access teams use Wi-Fi devices.
- **T1528 – Steal Application Access Token:** "Authentic Antics" steals Microsoft 365 OAuth tokens/credentials.

### Discovery
- **T1083 – File and Directory Discovery:** Uses Forfiles to locate PDF/Excel/Word files; searched a compromised DCCC host for specific terms.

### Lateral Movement
- **T1021.002 – Remote Services: SMB/Windows Admin Shares:** Maps drives with net + admin creds; used SMB in Nearest Neighbor (C0051).
- **T1210 – Exploitation of Remote Services:** Exploits to spread internally.

### Collection
- **T1005 – Data from Local System:** Retrieves internal documents, staging with Forfiles.
- **T1113 – Screen Capture:** X-Agent/X-Tunnel modules capture screenshots.
- **T1114 – Email Collection:** Harvests mailbox contents (Exchange/M365).

### Command and Control
- **T1071.001 – Application Layer Protocol: Web Protocols:** CHOPSTICK blends HTTP/HTTPS and legitimate channels.
- **T1090.002 / .003 – Proxy: External / Multi-hop:** Relays C2 through compromised hosts and over Tor/VPN.
- **T1573.001 – Encrypted Channel: Symmetric Cryptography:** Delphi backdoor (Zebrocy/Downdelph) uses custom symmetric crypto.

## Tooling & Malware
- **X-Agent / CHOPSTICK / XAgentOSX (S0023):** Flagship modular cross-platform backdoor (keylogging, file theft, screenshots); used vs. DNC.
- **X-Tunnel (S0117):** Network proxy/tunneling tool to pivot and exfiltrate from internal networks.
- **JHUHUGIT / GAMEFISH (S0044):** First-stage dropper/downloader, often via Office exploits.
- **Zebrocy (S0251):** Multi-language downloader-backdoor for recon and follow-on payloads.
- **Downdelph (S0134):** Rare Delphi implant deployed with a bootkit for stealthy persistence.
- **CORESHELL / Sofacy (S0137):** Downloader that installs further payloads.
- **ADVSTORESHELL (S0045):** Modular backdoor for recon and command execution.
- **LoJax (S0397):** First in-the-wild UEFI rootkit — firmware-level persistence surviving OS reinstall.
- **Drovorub:** Linux malware toolset (kernel-module rootkit + agent + server) exposed by NSA/FBI in 2020.
- **GooseEgg:** Post-compromise tool exploiting CVE-2022-38028 (Print Spooler) for SYSTEM-level cred theft (Microsoft, Apr 2024).
- **VPNFilter:** Modular router/NAS implant infecting 500k+ devices worldwide (2018).
- **Cannon / Koadic (S0250):** Cannon — email-based C2 trojan; Koadic — open-source JScript/VBScript C2 framework.
- **Authentic Antics:** Outlook-process malware spoofing login prompts to steal M365 credentials/OAuth tokens (NCSC, Jul 2025).
- **Mimikatz (S0002) / Responder (S0174):** Cred-dumping and LLMNR/NBT-NS poisoning.

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **2008** — Operations against Georgia during the Russo-Georgian war.
- **2015 (Apr)** — TV5Monde: French broadcaster knocked off air via "CyberCaliphate" front.
- **2015 (Apr–May)** — German Bundestag: ~16 GB exfiltrated; basis for 2020 EU/UK sanctions.
- **2016** — DNC / DCCC / Clinton campaign hack-and-leak (basis for July 2018 indictment).
- **2016 (Aug)** — WADA / "Fancy Bears" leaks of athlete medical records.
- **2018** — VPNFilter router botnet (FBI disruption); DOJ indictments (July & October).
- **2018–19** — LoJax UEFI rootkit discovered (ESET); Zebrocy campaigns across Europe/Central Asia.
- **2020** — NSA/FBI expose Drovorub Linux malware; "Global Brute Force" password-spray campaign (reported 2021).
- **2022 (Apr–Dec)** — Exploitation of Outlook CVE-2023-23397 (NTLM-relay/credential theft) across Europe.
- **2022–2025** — Western logistics & technology targeting supporting Ukraine aid (CISA AA25-141A / ACSC, May 21 2025); also targeting internet-facing IP cameras at border crossings.
- **2024 (Apr)** — Microsoft details GooseEgg / CVE-2022-38028.
- **2024 (Nov)** — "Nearest Neighbor" attack (Volexity): daisy-chained nearby Wi-Fi networks for covert access (campaign C0051).
- **2025 (Jul)** — NCSC exposes "Authentic Antics"; UK sanctions GRU units and 18 individuals.

## Detection & Hunting
- **Email/identity:** Hunt Exchange/M365 audit logs for mailbox-permission changes (Add-MailboxPermission, Add-MailboxFolderPermission), suspicious OAuth/token grants, anomalous EWS/Graph access (T1098, T1528, T1114).
- **CVE-2023-23397:** Inspect Exchange items for crafted PidLidReminderFileParameter UNC paths; alert on outbound SMB/NTLM auth to external IPs.
- **Print Spooler / GooseEgg:** Detect suspicious DLLs/JS-constraints files, rundll32 spawning from spooler context, unexpected SYSTEM processes (CVE-2022-38028).
- **Auth logs:** Password-spray/brute-force patterns from Tor exit nodes and commercial VPN ranges (T1110, T1133).
- **Credential theft:** LSASS access from non-standard processes; Mimikatz signatures; LLMNR/NBT-NS (Responder) anomalies (T1003.001, T1040).
- **C2/execution:** rundll32 loading DLLs from C:\Windows\*; DDE-launched PowerShell; HTTP/HTTPS beaconing; Tor/multi-hop egress (T1218.011, T1559.002, T1071.001, T1090).
- **Firmware:** UEFI integrity monitoring / SPI-flash scanning for LoJax; alert on Computrace/LoJack agent presence.
- **Wi-Fi (Nearest Neighbor):** Audit dual-homed hosts bridging to internal SSIDs; enforce certificate-based Wi-Fi access.
- **IOCs:** Pull current hashes/domains/IPs from CISA AA25-141A, NSA Drovorub/Brute-Force advisories, Microsoft/Volexity reports (rotate frequently).

## Mitigations
- Enforce phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/hardware keys) everywhere.
- Patch the exploited CVEs urgently: CVE-2023-23397 (Outlook), CVE-2022-38028 (Print Spooler), Exchange flaws, edge/VPN CVEs.
- Harden M365/Exchange: restrict mailbox-permission delegation, audit OAuth consent, enable Conditional Access and impossible-travel alerts.
- Disable/restrict legacy auth and NTLM; segment networks; restrict SMB; disable Print Spooler where unneeded.
- Disable LLMNR/NBT-NS to defeat Responder-style poisoning.
- Email hardening: attachment sandboxing, block internet macros, disable DDE auto-execute.
- Secure Boot / UEFI write protection and firmware hygiene against LoJax-class rootkits.
- Wi-Fi: certificate-based enterprise auth, prevent dual-homed bridging.
- Edge devices: patch and credential-rotate routers, VPNs, IP cameras; remove default creds.
- Centralized logging with long retention; apply ACSC Essential Eight (Australian entities).

## Activity Log
<!-- Append-only: future intel sweeps add dated entries below -->
- _(seed)_ 2026-06-17 — Profile created.

## Trend Tracking
| Date | Event Type | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| | | | |

## References
- MITRE ATT&CK Group G0007: https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0007/
- MITRE Nearest Neighbor Campaign C0051: https://attack.mitre.org/campaigns/C0051/
- CISA AA25-141A (GRU targeting logistics/tech): https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa25-141a
- ACSC / ASD advisory: https://www.cyber.gov.au/about-us/view-all-content/news/russian-gru-targeting-western-logistics-entities-and-technology-companies
- ASD Annual Cyber Threat Report 2024-25: https://www.cyber.gov.au/about-us/view-all-content/reports-and-statistics/annual-cyber-threat-report-2024-2025
- Microsoft – GooseEgg / CVE-2022-38028: https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2024/04/22/analyzing-forest-blizzards-custom-post-compromise-tool-for-exploiting-cve-2022-38028-to-obtain-credentials/
- NSA/FBI Drovorub advisory: https://media.defense.gov/2020/Aug/13/2002476465/-1/-1/0/CSA_DROVORUB_RUSSIAN_GRU_MALWARE_AUG_2020.PDF
- DOJ 2018 indictment (GRU): https://cdn.cnn.com/cnn/2018/images/07/13/gru.indictment.pdf
- EU Council Bundestag sanctions (Oct 22, 2020): https://www.consilium.europa.eu/en/press/press-releases/2020/10/22/malicious-cyber-attacks-eu-sanctions-two-individuals-and-one-body-over-2015-bundestag-hack/
- UK NCSC – Authentic Antics: https://www.ncsc.gov.uk/news/uk-call-out-russian-military-intelligence-use-espionage-tool
- Volexity – Nearest Neighbor: https://www.volexity.com/blog/2024/11/22/the-nearest-neighbor-attack-how-a-russian-apt-weaponized-nearby-wi-fi-networks-for-covert-access/

# APT29 — APT Profile

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | Russia — SVR (Foreign Intelligence Service) |
| Aliases | Cozy Bear, The Dukes, Midnight Blizzard (ex-NOBELIUM), UNC2452, Dark Halo |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G0016 |
| First seen | 2008 |
| Status | Active |
| Motivation | Espionage |

## Overview
APT29 is a Russian state-sponsored cyber-espionage group attributed to the SVR (Foreign Intelligence Service). Active since at least 2008, it is among the most sophisticated and disciplined intrusion sets observed, distinguished by exceptional operational security, patient long-term access, and a steady evolution from on-premises tradecraft to cloud- and identity-centric operations.

The group is responsible for landmark campaigns including the 2015–2016 DNC intrusion, the 2020 SolarWinds supply-chain compromise (one of the most consequential espionage operations in history), COVID-19 vaccine research targeting, and a sustained series of cloud/identity attacks against Microsoft, HPE, and government and diplomatic targets through 2024–2025.

APT29 increasingly avoids on-host malware in favor of abusing valid accounts, OAuth tokens, federation trust, and cloud administration capabilities — making behavioral and identity analytics essential to detection. It is not financially motivated; objectives are strategic intelligence collection for the Russian state.

## Attribution & Origin
- **Country / sponsor:** Russia — SVR (Foreign Intelligence Service). Assessed "almost certainly part of the SVR."
- **April 15, 2021:** US and UK formally attributed the SolarWinds compromise to the SVR/APT29. The same day the US issued Executive Order 14024 and OFAC sanctioned Russian entities/individuals naming the SVR's role; the White House also tied APT29/Cozy Bear to the 2016 DNC hack. UK NCSC co-attributed.
- **Five Eyes + partners:** The Feb 2024 cloud-tactics advisory (AA24-057A) is co-sealed by NSA, CISA, FBI, CNMF, UK NCSC, Australia's ASD/ACSC, Canada's CCCS, and New Zealand's GCSB — all agreeing APT29 = SVR.
- **EU context:** The EU maintains cyber-sanctions frameworks used against Russian intelligence cyber operations; the cleanest dated, named action tied to SolarWinds is the US EO 14024 / OFAC package of April 15, 2021.

## Motivation & Objectives
Strategic cyber-espionage / intelligence collection for the Russian state. Long-term, stealthy access to governments, foreign-policy/diplomatic targets, defense, think tanks, and (since 2020) cloud/identity infrastructure. Not financially motivated; emphasis on persistence, OPSEC, and collecting email/communications and sensitive policy data.

## Target Profile
**Sectors:** Government/foreign ministries and diplomatic missions, NATO-member networks, think tanks and research institutes, defense, IT/cloud and managed service providers (for downstream access), healthcare/pharma (COVID-vaccine research, 2020), energy, education, law enforcement, and military.

**Regions:** Primarily Europe, NATO members, and the US. Diplomatic spear-phishing has hit embassies in Azerbaijan, Greece, Romania, and Italy (2023 WinRAR campaign).

**APAC / Australia targeting:** Yes. Australia's ASD/ACSC is a co-author/co-sealer of the key SVR advisories — most notably AA24-057A *"SVR Cyber Actors Adapt Tactics for Initial Cloud Access"* (Feb 2024) and the earlier 2020 COVID-vaccine targeting advisory. ACSC re-publishes these via cyber.gov.au, indicating Australian-relevant exposure especially for cloud/M365-hosted government and research organizations. Public reporting frames APAC/Australia exposure mainly through these joint advisories and cloud-tenant targeting rather than a standalone Australia-only victim disclosure.

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK

### Reconnaissance / Resource Development
- **T1595.002 – Active Scanning: Vulnerability Scanning:** Widespread scanning to find exploitable vulns.
- **T1583.001 / .006 – Acquire Infrastructure (Domains / Web Services):** Bought C2 domains via resellers; abused Dropbox, Constant Contact, and Twitter (HAMMERTOSS) for C2.

### Initial Access
- **T1195.002 – Supply Chain Compromise (Software):** Trojanized SolarWinds Orion updates (SUNBURST) for mass initial access.
- **T1566.001 / .002 / .003 – Phishing (Attachment / Link / via Service):** Spear-phishing with malicious docs/.lnk/ISO; used Constant Contact to send lures.
- **T1078.004 – Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts:** Logs into cloud tenants with compromised/dormant credentials — current signature TTP.
- **T1199 – Trusted Relationship:** Compromised IT/cloud/MSP providers (and Mimecast certs) to reach downstream customers.
- **T1190 / T1133 – Exploit Public-Facing App / External Remote Services:** Exploited internet-facing services (JetBrains TeamCity CVE-2023-42793).

### Execution
- **T1059.001 / .003 / .005 / .009 – Command & Scripting Interpreter (PowerShell / cmd / VBScript / Cloud API):** PowerShell for AD recon and exfil; Sibot in VBScript; Microsoft Graph API / AADInternals for M365 actions.
- **T1651 – Cloud Administration Command:** Azure Run Command and Admin-on-Behalf-of (AOBO) to run code on VMs.

### Persistence
- **T1098.001 / .002 / .003 / .005 – Account Manipulation:** Added creds to OAuth apps/service principals, granted mailbox-impersonation rights, enrolled attacker devices/MFA.
- **T1556.007 – Modify Authentication Process: Hybrid Identity:** MagicWeb — malicious DLL into the AD FS process to authenticate as any user.
- **T1547.001 / T1053.005 / T1505.003 – Run keys / Scheduled Tasks / Web Shells:** Registry Run keys, named and hijacked scheduled tasks, Exchange web shells.

### Privilege Escalation
- **T1548.002 – Bypass UAC.**
- **T1484.002 – Domain/Tenant Policy Modification: Trust Modification (Golden SAML):** Reconfigured Azure AD federation to trust attacker-controlled SAML signing certs.

### Defense Evasion
- **T1562.001 – Impair Defenses / Disable Logging:** Used AUDITPOL; disabled M365 Purview Audit before mailbox theft.
- **T1027.006 / T1553.005 – HTML Smuggling / Mark-of-the-Web Bypass:** ISO-in-HTML smuggling to evade MOTW.
- **T1550.001 / .003 / .004 – Use Alternate Authentication Material (app tokens / pass-the-ticket / web session cookie):** Stolen tokens and forged duo-sid cookie to bypass MFA.

### Credential Access
- **T1110.001 / .003 – Brute Force (Password Guessing / Spraying):** Low-and-slow password spray (the 2024 Microsoft breach vector).
- **T1621 – Multi-Factor Authentication Request Generation ("MFA bombing").**
- **T1003.002 / .004 / .006 – OS Credential Dumping (SAM / LSA Secrets / DCSync).**
- **T1606 – Forge Web Credentials (Golden SAML)** and **T1552.004 – Unsecured Credentials: Private Keys** (extracted AD FS SAML signing key material).
- **T1649 – Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates:** Abused misconfigured AD CS templates.
- **T1539 – Steal Web Session Cookie:** Copied Chrome profiles.

### Discovery
- **T1087.002 / .004 – Account Discovery (Domain / Cloud):** Get-ADUser, Azure AD enumeration.
- **T1482 – Domain Trust Discovery; T1018 – Remote System Discovery** (AdFind).

### Lateral Movement
- **T1021.001 – Remote Services: RDP** from public-facing to internal servers.
- **T1550.003 – Pass-the-Ticket / Kerberos** abuse.

### Collection
- **T1114.002 – Remote Email Collection:** EWS/Graph mailbox theft; New-MailboxExportRequest for executives/IT staff.
- **T1560.001 – Archive via Utility:** 7-Zip into password-protected archives pre-exfil.

### Command and Control
- **T1071.001 – Web Protocols (HTTP); T1573 – Encrypted Channel; T1568 – Dynamic Resolution; T1090.003 / .004 – Multi-hop Proxy / Domain Fronting (Tor + meek).**

### Exfiltration
- **T1048 – Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol** (and HTTP-based exfil during SolarWinds).

## Tooling & Malware
- **SUNBURST / Solorigate (S0559):** Trojanized SolarWinds Orion DLL backdoor; signed via stolen code-signing cert.
- **SUNSPOT (S0562):** Build-server implant that injected SUNBURST into the Orion build pipeline.
- **TEARDROP (S0560) / RAINDROP:** Memory-only droppers delivering Cobalt Strike post-SolarWinds.
- **GoldMax / GoldFinder / Sibot:** Second-stage NOBELIUM tools (C2, connectivity check, VBScript loader).
- **WellMess:** Lightweight Golang/.NET implant (shell commands, file transfer); COVID-vaccine campaign.
- **WellMail:** Golang tool to run commands/scripts and exfiltrate to hardcoded C2; COVID-vaccine campaign.
- **FoggyWeb:** Stealthy AD FS backdoor to exfiltrate token-signing/config secrets and load payloads.
- **MagicWeb:** AD FS post-compromise DLL that manipulates SAML auth certs to sign in as any user.
- **GraphicalProton (GraphDrop / SPICYBEAT):** Loader using OneDrive/Graph for C2 (TeamCity/diplomatic activity).
- **HAMMERTOSS (S0037):** Classic Duke backdoor using Twitter/GitHub/cloud for C2.
- **Commodity/offensive tooling:** Cobalt Strike, Mimikatz, AdFind, SDelete, Tor + meek (domain fronting), 7-Zip.

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **2008–2015** — "The Dukes" era: long-running espionage vs. governments/think tanks (CozyDuke, MiniDuke, HAMMERTOSS).
- **2015–2016** — DNC intrusion (Cozy Bear).
- **2019** — Operation Ghost (C0023): continued Dukes operations using social-media C2 and steganography.
- **2020** — COVID-19 vaccine R&D targeting: WellMess/WellMail against vaccine researchers (UK/US/Canada).
- **2020** — SolarWinds Compromise (Campaign C0024): supply-chain backdoor (SUNBURST) → ~18,000 customers, targeted follow-on; Golden SAML/cloud-token abuse. Public attribution April 2021.
- **2021** — USAID/Constant Contact phishing (NOBELIUM); FoggyWeb/MagicWeb AD FS persistence disclosed.
- **2023** — JetBrains TeamCity (CVE-2023-42793): large-scale exploitation from Sept 2023.
- **2023** — WinRAR zero-day (CVE-2023-38831): diplomatic spear-phishing on European embassies with "BMW for sale" lures.
- **2023–2024** — HPE & Microsoft corporate breaches: Midnight Blizzard accessed HPE cloud email from ~May 2023; Microsoft corporate email breach detected Jan 12, 2024 via low-and-slow password spray on a legacy non-MFA test tenant → OAuth abuse to reach senior staff/source mailboxes.
- **2024** — AA24-057A cloud-tactics advisory (Five Eyes incl. ACSC): documents the shift to cloud-native tradecraft.
- **2024–2025** — Continued identity/cloud espionage; CISA's Jan 2025 Microsoft Expanded Cloud Logs Implementation Playbook released to aid hunting.

## Detection & Hunting
**Cloud / M365 / Entra ID (highest priority):**
- Enable and hunt M365 Expanded Cloud Logs (MailItemsAccessed, Graph activity) per CISA's 2025 playbook.
- Alert on new credentials/secrets added to OAuth apps and service principals (T1098.001); new app role grants; consent to unusual enterprise apps.
- Hunt new device registrations / MFA enrollment on dormant or recently password-sprayed accounts (T1098.005).
- Detect federation/trust changes (Set-MsolDomainFederationSettings, new SAML signing certs) — Golden SAML indicator (T1484.002).
- Monitor for Purview/unified audit log being disabled on a mailbox right before access.
- Sign-in anomalies: impossible travel, residential-proxy/VPS ASNs, token replay, legacy-auth/non-interactive sign-ins.
- Password-spray signatures: many accounts, few attempts each, single source over time; failed→success on no-MFA accounts.
- Hunt EWS/Graph mass mailbox export (New-MailboxExportRequest, Mail.Read/Mail.ReadWrite grants, ApplicationImpersonation).

**On-prem / endpoint:**
- AD FS host integrity: unexpected DLLs loaded into Microsoft.IdentityServer.Servicehost (MagicWeb), changes to its .config (T1556.007).
- reg save of SAM/SECURITY hives, DCSync replication from non-DC accounts (T1003.006), Kerberoasting.
- Scheduled-task creation/modification then revert; netsh advfirewall and auditpol tampering.
- 7-Zip password-protected archive creation in staging dirs; HTML/ISO smuggling; Tor/meek/domain-fronting C2.

**IOC sources:** AA24-057A, NCSC "Further TTPs associated with SVR cyber actors," Microsoft/Mandiant blogs, FBI IC3 PSA. Treat hashes/domains as time-limited; prioritize behavioral/identity analytics.

## Mitigations
- Enforce phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2/cert-based) on all accounts — including legacy/test/service tenants (the Microsoft-breach lesson).
- Eliminate dormant accounts; rotate/limit service-principal and OAuth-app secrets; audit app consent and admin-consent workflows.
- Least privilege + Conditional Access; restrict legacy authentication; shorten token/session lifetimes.
- Monitor and lock down federation trust and AD FS; migrate off AD FS to cloud-native auth where possible; protect AD CS templates (mitigate ESC misconfigs, T1649).
- Deploy canary/honeytoken accounts that are never legitimately used.
- Patch internet-facing software promptly (TeamCity CVE-2023-42793, WinRAR CVE-2023-38831).
- Centralize and protect logs (prevent audit disablement); enable expanded cloud logging by default.
- Network egress controls and proxy inspection to surface domain-fronting/Tor C2.

## Activity Log

- CISA AA23-347A (SVR exploiting JetBrains TeamCity): https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa23-347a

# APT33 — APT Profile

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | Iran (assessed IRGC-aligned) |
| Aliases | Elfin, HOLMIUM, Peach Sandstorm, Refined Kitten, MAGNALLIUM, TA451 |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G0064 |
| First seen | 2013 |
| Status | Active |
| Motivation | Espionage, pre-positioning for sabotage/destruction |

## Overview
APT33 is a suspected Iranian state-sponsored threat group active since at least 2013. It is best known for cyber-espionage against the aviation/aerospace and energy sectors, and for its operational links to destructive wiper activity. While much of APT33's documented activity is collection-focused, its overlap with Shamoon-style destructive tooling (DROPSHOT/SHAPESHIFT) and its targeting of petrochemical and critical-infrastructure organizations have led analysts to assess that a portion of its operations supports pre-positioning for potential sabotage. Microsoft tracks the group as Peach Sandstorm and has documented its pivot toward large-scale password-spraying campaigns and, more recently, custom backdoors (e.g., Tickler-class tooling) against high-value Western targets.

## Attribution & Origin
APT33 is assessed to operate on behalf of the Iranian government, with reporting linking it to the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC). Attribution rests on: targeting that aligns with Iranian strategic interests (regional rivals such as Saudi Arabia, the aviation and energy sectors); Farsi-language artifacts and Iranian time-zone working hours observed in tooling and infrastructure; and infrastructure/tooling overlaps with other Iran-nexus destructive activity. FireEye first publicly profiled the group in 2017. Vendor naming maps as: Peach Sandstorm (Microsoft), Elfin (Symantec), Refined Kitten (CrowdStrike), HOLMIUM (legacy Microsoft), MAGNALLIUM (Dragos).

## Motivation & Objectives
- **Espionage / intelligence collection** against aviation, aerospace, defense, and energy organizations to support Iran's military and economic objectives.
- **Pre-positioning for sabotage/destruction** — links to wiper malware (DROPSHOT/SHAPESHIFT, in the Shamoon family lineage) suggest contingency capability against critical infrastructure.
- **Strategic competitor monitoring**, particularly of Gulf-state rivals (Saudi Arabia) and Western aviation supply chains.

## Target Profile
- **Sectors:** Aviation/aerospace (commercial and military), energy, oil & gas, petrochemical, defense industrial base, government, and manufacturing.
- **Regions:** Heavy focus on Saudi Arabia and the broader Middle East; significant targeting of the United States and South Korea; expanding activity across Europe.
- **APAC / Australia note:** Aviation and energy supply-chain targeting brings APAC-based engineering, defense-contractor, and energy firms into scope. Australian defense, aerospace, and critical-infrastructure organizations — and multinationals with Gulf operations — fall within APT33's collection interests, particularly where they intersect aviation supply chains and oil & gas.

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK
**Reconnaissance / Resource Development**
- T1588 — Obtain Capabilities (commodity RATs, exploit acquisition)

**Initial Access**
- T1566 — Phishing (spearphishing with job-themed lures, fake recruitment domains)
- T1110 — Brute Force (notably password spraying at scale — Peach Sandstorm)
- T1078 — Valid Accounts (reuse of harvested/sprayed credentials)

**Execution**
- T1059 — Command and Scripting Interpreter (PowerShell, VBScript, AutoIt)
- T1204 — User Execution (malicious attachments/links)
- T1203 — Exploitation for Client Execution (e.g., CVE-2017-11774 Outlook Home Page abuse)

**Persistence / Privilege Escalation**
- T1547 — Boot or Logon Autostart Execution (Registry Run keys)
- T1546 — Event Triggered Execution
- T1053 — Scheduled Task/Job
- T1068 — Exploitation for Privilege Escalation

**Defense Evasion**
- T1027 — Obfuscated/Encoded files and information

**Credential Access**
- T1003 — OS Credential Dumping
- T1555 — Credentials from Password Stores
- T1552 — Unsecured Credentials

**Command and Control**
- T1071 — Application Layer Protocol
- T1573 — Encrypted Channel
- T1132 — Data Encoding
- T1571 — Non-Standard Port
- T1105 — Ingress Tool Transfer
- T1040 — Network Sniffing

**Collection / Exfiltration**
- T1560 — Archive Collected Data
- T1048 — Exfiltration Over Alternative Protocol

**ICS (where critical-infrastructure intrusions observed)**
- T0852 — Screen Capture; T0853 — Scripting; T0865 — Spearphishing Attachment

## Tooling & Malware
- **TURNEDUP** — custom backdoor (download/upload, command exec, reverse shell).
- **DROPSHOT / SHAPESHIFT** — dropper and wiper linked to the Shamoon destructive family.
- **Commodity RATs** — NanoCore, NetWire, Remcos, used to lower attribution and cost.
- **Offensive frameworks** — PowerShell Empire, PoshC2 for post-exploitation and C2.
- **AutoIt-based loaders/backdoors** and PowerShell stagers for initial footholds.
- Heavy use of **fake recruitment / aviation-themed domains** for credential harvesting and lure delivery.

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **2013–2016** — Early espionage operations against aviation and petrochemical targets in the Middle East, US, and South Korea.
- **2017 (Sep)** — FireEye publicly profiles APT33; documents spearphishing using aviation job lures and TURNEDUP backdoor.
- **2018** — Exploitation of CVE-2017-11774 (Outlook Home Page) for persistence; continued aviation/energy targeting.
- **2019** — Symantec ("Elfin") reports ~50 organizations targeted across Saudi Arabia and the US; password-spraying at scale.
- **2019 (Aug)** — Microsoft disrupts HOLMIUM infrastructure; notes targeting of industrial control system supply chains.
- **2023 (Sep)** — Microsoft details Peach Sandstorm password-spray campaigns and custom backdoor deployment against defense, satellite, and pharma sectors.
- **2024** — Continued Peach Sandstorm activity, including the Tickler custom backdoor against US/UAE government, defense, and satellite organizations using Azure infrastructure.

## Detection & Hunting
- **Password spraying:** Alert on high-volume failed auths across many accounts from few source IPs; monitor for "low-and-slow" lockout-evasion patterns against O365/Entra ID and VPN portals.
- **CVE-2017-11774:** Hunt for unexpected Outlook `WebView`/Home Page registry modifications under `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Office\...\Outlook\WebView`.
- **Commodity RATs:** Maintain detections for NanoCore/NetWire/Remcos C2 patterns and known config artifacts.
- **PowerShell:** Enable Script Block Logging (Event ID 4104); hunt encoded/`-enc` PowerShell, Empire/PoshC2 stager signatures.
- **Infrastructure:** Watch for newly registered aviation/recruitment-themed domains and Azure tenant abuse for C2.

## Mitigations
- Enforce phishing-resistant MFA on all external auth surfaces; deploy conditional access and impossible-travel/risk-based policies to blunt password spraying.
- Patch Outlook/Office (CVE-2017-11774) and prioritize internet-facing service patching.
- Application allowlisting and constrained-language-mode PowerShell to limit script execution.
- Block/alert on commodity-RAT C2 and known APT33 infrastructure via threat-intel feeds.
- Segment ICS/OT from IT networks; restrict and monitor engineering workstations given destructive-tooling risk.

# APT34 (OilRig) — APT Profile

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | Iran (assessed MOIS-aligned) |
| Aliases | OilRig, Helix Kitten, Cobalt Gypsy, Evasive Serpens, Earth Simnavaz, Crambus, Hazel Sandstorm |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G0049 |
| First seen | 2014 |
| Status | Active |
| Motivation | Espionage / long-term intelligence collection |

## Overview
APT34 (OilRig) is a long-running Iranian cyber-espionage group active since at least 2014, widely assessed to operate on behalf of Iran's Ministry of Intelligence and Security (MOIS). It is one of the most prolific Iran-nexus actors in the Middle East, specializing in supply-chain-style trust abuse, DNS-based command and control, and custom backdoors delivered via spearphishing and compromised credentials. OilRig is notable for its tradecraft maturity: it develops bespoke .NET/PowerShell toolsets, abuses legitimate services (DNS, web mail, cloud) for covert C2 and exfiltration, and conducts patient, persistent operations against government and energy targets. The group has continued to evolve, deploying the STEALHOOK backdoor against Gulf-state Exchange servers and exploiting privilege-escalation CVEs in recent campaigns.

## Attribution & Origin
OilRig is attributed to Iran (assessed MOIS) based on: Iranian infrastructure and Farsi-language artifacts in tooling; targeting tightly aligned with Iranian regional intelligence priorities (Gulf states, Israel, regional government and energy entities); and operational overlaps with the 2019 "Lab Dookhtegan" leaks that exposed OilRig tooling, victims, and operator identities. Microsoft tracks it as Hazel Sandstorm (formerly EUROPIUM); CrowdStrike as Helix Kitten; Secureworks as Cobalt Gypsy; Palo Alto Unit 42 as Evasive Serpens; Trend Micro as Earth Simnavaz; Symantec as Crambus.

## Motivation & Objectives
- **Strategic espionage** — collection of government, diplomatic, military, and economic intelligence in the Middle East.
- **Critical-sector access** — persistent footholds in energy, oil & gas, and telecommunications to support Iranian state interests.
- **Supply-chain and trust abuse** — compromising one organization to phish or pivot into partner/government targets.
- No financial or destructive motive in primary reporting; focus is durable, covert collection.

## Target Profile
- **Sectors:** Government, energy/oil & gas, financial services, telecommunications, chemical, aviation, and broader critical infrastructure.
- **Regions:** Concentrated in the Gulf (UAE, Saudi Arabia, Qatar, Kuwait), Israel, Lebanon, and the wider Middle East; also the United States and Europe.
- **APAC / Australia note:** OilRig's telecommunications and energy targeting, plus its supply-chain trust abuse, places APAC telcos, energy multinationals, and government-linked contractors at risk. Australian organizations with Gulf-region joint ventures, energy operations, or shared service providers are plausible secondary targets via partner-compromise and credential reuse.

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK
**Initial Access**
- T1566 — Phishing (spearphishing links/attachments; fake job/LinkedIn lures)
- T1190 — Exploit Public-Facing Application (e.g., Exchange, web servers)
- T1078 — Valid Accounts (harvested/compromised credentials)
- T1133 — External Remote Services

**Execution**
- T1059 — Command and Scripting Interpreter (PowerShell, VBScript, .NET)
- T1204 — User Execution

**Persistence**
- T1505 — Server Software Component (web shells, e.g., TwoFace)
- T1547 — Boot or Logon Autostart Execution
- T1543 — Create or Modify System Process (services)
- T1136 — Create Account
- T1098 — Account Manipulation

**Privilege Escalation / Defense Evasion**
- T1574 — Hijack Execution Flow (DLL side-loading)
- T1027 — Obfuscated Files or Information
- T1070 — Indicator Removal
- T1562 — Impair Defenses

**Credential Access**
- T1003 — OS Credential Dumping (Mimikatz, LaZagne)
- T1555 — Credentials from Password Stores (VALUEVAULT — browser creds)
- T1056 — Input Capture (keylogging)
- T1558 — Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets

**Command and Control**
- T1071 — Application Layer Protocol (DNS, HTTP/S, web mail)
- T1572 — Protocol Tunneling (DNS tunneling — hallmark TTP)
- T1102 — Web Service (legitimate cloud services for C2)
- T1573 — Encrypted Channel
- T1105 — Ingress Tool Transfer

**Lateral Movement / Collection / Exfiltration**
- T1021 — Remote Services
- T1567 — Exfiltration Over Web Service
- T1041 — Exfiltration Over C2 Channel

## Tooling & Malware
- **Helminth** — early VBScript/PowerShell backdoor with HTTP and DNS C2.
- **QUADAGENT / ISMAgent / ISMInjector** — PowerShell/.NET backdoor family using DNS and HTTP C2.
- **TONEDEAF / VALUEVAULT** — HTTP backdoor and Golang browser-credential stealer (LinkedIn "Top secret" campaign).
- **TwoFace** — ASP.NET web shell for persistence and lateral tasking.
- **RDAT** — backdoor with novel email-based (Exchange Web Services + steganography) C2.
- **SideTwist / Karkoff / DNSpionage** — additional custom backdoors with DNS-centric C2.
- **STEALHOOK** — recent backdoor exfiltrating credentials from on-prem Exchange (Earth Simnavaz, 2024).
- **Mimikatz, LaZagne, certutil, PsExec** and other living-off-the-land / public tools.

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **2014–2016** — Early Helminth campaigns against Gulf government and financial targets; DNS-tunneling C2 established as a signature.
- **2017** — TwoFace web shell and ISMAgent operations; broad Middle East government/energy targeting.
- **2018** — DNSpionage campaign hijacking DNS records of government and telecom entities (linked cluster), prompting a US CISA emergency directive on DNS tampering.
- **2019 (Apr)** — "Lab Dookhtegan" Telegram leak exposes OilRig source code, victim lists, and operator data.
- **2019 (Jul)** — TONEDEAF/VALUEVAULT campaign using a fake "Top secret" LinkedIn persona and Cambridge-themed lures.
- **2020** — RDAT backdoor using steganographic email C2 against a Middle East telecom.
- **2021–2023** — SideTwist, Karkoff, and Saitama backdoors; continued Gulf government targeting (Crambus/Earth Simnavaz reporting).
- **2024** — Earth Simnavaz deploys STEALHOOK and exploits CVE-2024-30088 (Windows privilege escalation) against UAE/Gulf government and energy entities, abusing on-prem Exchange for exfiltration.

## Detection & Hunting
- **DNS tunneling:** Alert on anomalous DNS volume, high-entropy/long subdomain labels, and TXT-record-heavy traffic; baseline normal DNS per host.
- **Web shells:** Hunt for new/modified ASP.NET/ASPX files in web roots; monitor w3wp.exe spawning cmd/powershell (TwoFace behavior).
- **Exchange abuse:** Monitor EWS access patterns, anomalous mailbox rules, and credential exfiltration from on-prem Exchange (STEALHOOK).
- **Credential theft:** Detect Mimikatz/LaZagne behavior and browser-credential access (VALUEVAULT).
- **PowerShell:** Script Block Logging; hunt obfuscated/DNS-aware PowerShell stagers.
- **Infrastructure:** Watch for DLL side-loading via legitimate signed binaries and use of cloud/web services as C2.

## Mitigations
- Deploy DNS monitoring/filtering and restrict outbound DNS to approved resolvers; consider DNS firewall/RPZ.
- Patch internet-facing applications promptly (Exchange, web servers) and prioritize privilege-escalation CVEs (e.g., CVE-2024-30088).
- Enforce phishing-resistant MFA; monitor for malicious inbox rules and account manipulation.
- Web-server hardening, file-integrity monitoring on web roots, and least-privilege service accounts.
- Network segmentation, credential-tiering, and Kerberos hardening to limit lateral movement.

| | | | |

- https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/behind-the-scenes-with-oilrig/

# APT40 (Leviathan) — APT Profile

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | PRC Ministry of State Security (MSS), Hainan State Security Department; front company Hainan Xiandun Technology |
| Aliases | Leviathan, TEMP.Periscope, TEMP.Jumper, Kryptonite Panda, Gingham Typhoon, Bronze Mohawk, MUDCARP, Gadolinium |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G0065 |
| First seen | 2009 |
| Status | Active |
| Motivation | Espionage (state) |

## Overview
APT40 (MITRE name "Leviathan") is a China-nexus cyber-espionage group active since at least 2009, attributed to the PRC Ministry of State Security's Hainan State Security Department. The group is notable for **rapid weaponisation of newly disclosed vulnerabilities**, a strong preference for exploiting **public-facing applications** over phishing, heavy use of **web shells** for persistence, and the use of **compromised end-of-life SOHO devices as operational relay box (ORB) infrastructure**. It is the explicit subject of a July 2024 joint advisory **led by Australia's ACSC**.

## Attribution & Origin
- Attributed to the PRC **Ministry of State Security (MSS)**, specifically the **Hainan State Security Department (HSSD)** in Haikou, Hainan Province.
- **2021 DOJ indictment** (unsealed 19 July 2021; returned May 2021, S.D. California): "Four Chinese Nationals Working with the MSS Charged with Global Computer Intrusion Campaign."
  - **Ding Xiaoyang, Cheng Qingmin, Zhu Yunmin** — HSSD intelligence officers who coordinated/managed hackers and linguists.
  - **Wu Shurong** — hacker/malware author at the front company who carried out and supervised CNE operations.
  - Front company: **Hainan Xiandun Technology Development Co., Ltd. (海南仙盾)** — established to obscure the government's role; since disbanded; operated out of Haikou.
  - Charges: conspiracy to commit computer fraud and conspiracy to commit economic espionage; campaign timeframe charged roughly 2011–2018.

## Motivation & Objectives
State-sponsored cyber-espionage: theft of trade secrets, intellectual property, sensitive research, and government/defense information aligned with PRC strategic and economic priorities — including Belt and Road Initiative interests and maritime/naval technology.

## Target Profile
Sectors: academia/research institutes, aerospace/aviation, biomedical/healthcare, defense industrial base, government, manufacturing, **maritime/naval**, transportation (rail and shipping), education, robotics. Regions: United States, **Australia and the broader Indo-Pacific**, Canada, Europe, the Middle East, Southeast Asia, and the South China Sea area.

**July 2024 joint advisory — "APT40 Advisory: PRC MSS tradecraft in action" (AA24-190A).**
- **Lead author: Australian Signals Directorate's ACSC (ASD's ACSC).** Published 8–9 July 2024.
- **Co-sealing partners:** US CISA, NSA, FBI; UK NCSC; Canada CCCS; New Zealand NCSC; Germany (BND, BfV); Japan (NISC, NPA); Republic of Korea (NIS / NCSC).
- **Key emphasis:** APT40 *"has repeatedly targeted Australian networks as well as government and private sector networks in the region,"* and its tradecraft is regularly observed against **Australian** networks; the threat is ongoing.
- **Key technical findings:**
  - Rapid weaponisation of new vulnerabilities — adapts public POC exploits and deploys against vulnerable infrastructure within hours/days. Named exploited vulns: **Log4Shell (Log4j), Atlassian Confluence, Microsoft Exchange ProxyShell**.
  - Targets **end-of-life / unpatched SOHO devices** (home/small-office routers) as ORB infrastructure / last-hop C2 to blend with legitimate traffic.
  - Preference for **web shells** for early persistence on public-facing apps.
  - Heavy reliance on **exploiting public-facing applications** rather than phishing (a noted shift), plus extensive reconnaissance of target infrastructure.
  - **Two Australian case studies**: one ~April–May 2022 (public-facing app compromise, web-shell persistence) and one mid-August 2022 (ACSC incident response; exfiltration of privileged authentication credentials and network information). These correspond to MITRE Campaign **C0049 ("Leviathan Australian Intrusions")**.

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK
Technique IDs verified against the MITRE ATT&CK G0065 page and AA24-190A.

**Reconnaissance**
- `T1589.001` Gather Victim Identity Info: Credentials — collects compromised credentials for targeting.
- `T1594` Search Victim-Owned Websites — enumerates web-app resources for follow-on access.

**Resource Development**
- `T1584.008` Compromise Infrastructure: Network Devices — uses compromised SOHO devices as C2/ORB infrastructure.

**Initial Access**
- `T1190` Exploit Public-Facing Application — primary vector; rapid N-day/POC exploitation (Log4j, Confluence, ProxyShell).
- `T1133` External Remote Services — abuses VPNs/remote services for entry.
- `T1566.001` Spearphishing Attachment — historical phishing with user execution.

**Persistence**
- `T1505.003` Server Software Component: Web Shell — deploys web shells (e.g. China Chopper) on public-facing apps.
- `T1078.002` Valid Accounts: Domain Accounts — reuses compromised credentials for access and lateral movement.

**Credential Access**
- `T1003.001` OS Credential Dumping: LSASS Memory — dumps hashes via ProcDump, WCE, HOMEFRY.
- `T1111` Multi-Factor Authentication Interception — captures MFA token values from compromised appliances.

**Discovery / Lateral Movement**
- `T1018` Remote System Discovery and `T1482` Domain Trust Discovery — maps networks / AD enumeration.
- `T1021.002` Remote Services: SMB/Windows Admin Shares — lateral movement via remote shares.

**Command & Control / Exfiltration**
- `T1090.003` Proxy: Multi-hop Proxy and `T1572` Protocol Tunneling — conceals C2 source/infrastructure.
- `T1560` Archive Collected Data + `T1074.002` Remote Data Staging — stages/archives data prior to exfiltration.

## Tooling & Malware
- **Custom backdoors/utilities (MITRE Sxxxx):** Derusbi (S0021), BADFLICK (S0642), NanHaiShu (S0228), Orz/AIRBREAK (S0229), MURKYTOP (S0233), HOMEFRY (S0232, password dumper/cracker), BLACKCOFFEE (S0069, dead-drop resolvers), gh0st RAT (S0032).
- **Web shells / frameworks:** China Chopper (S0020), Cobalt Strike (S0154), Empire (S0363), PowerSploit (S0194).
- **Living-off-the-land utilities:** BITSAdmin (S0190), at (S0110), Net (S0039), Windows Credential Editor (S0005), Tor (S0183).

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **2009** — Earliest assessed APT40/Leviathan activity (MITRE/CISA).
- **2011–2018** — Period of the global intrusion campaign later charged in the 2021 DOJ indictment.
- **Oct 2017** — Proofpoint "Leviathan: Espionage actor spearphishes maritime and defense targets."
- **Mar 2018** — FireEye exposes TEMP.Periscope targeting US engineering and maritime industries.
- **Mar 2019** — FireEye "APT40: Examining a China-Nexus Espionage Actor"; Accenture iDefense "MUDCARP's Focus on Submarine Technologies."
- **19 Jul 2021** — DOJ unseals indictment of 4 MSS-HSSD actors (Hainan Xiandun); CISA/FBI/CNMF joint advisory **AA21-200A**.
- **Apr–May 2022 and mid-Aug 2022** — Two Australian intrusion case studies (MITRE Campaign **C0049**): web-shell persistence on a public-facing app; exfiltration of privileged credentials/network data during ACSC incident response.
- **8–9 Jul 2024** — ACSC-led international joint advisory **AA24-190A** "APT40 Advisory: PRC MSS tradecraft in action."

## Detection & Hunting
- **Patch promptly** — APT40 exploits new vulns within hours of POC release; treat newly disclosed CVEs on edge/public-facing apps as urgent.
- **Hunt for web shells** on public-facing apps; monitor anomalous processes spawned by web-server accounts and unexpected writes to web roots.
- Watch for unusual outbound to last-hop SOHO/residential IPs and multi-hop proxying/tunnelling.
- Alert on AD/trust enumeration bursts, credential-dumping artifacts (ProcDump/WCE/HOMEFRY), and reuse of valid accounts from anomalous sources.
- Comprehensive, centralised logging from internet-facing services, edge devices and authentication systems; retain and correlate for hunting.

## Mitigations
- **Replace end-of-life / unsupported equipment** — especially EOL SOHO routers and edge devices favored as ORB infrastructure.
- Enforce **phishing-resistant MFA** on all remote access (VPN, webmail, portals); monitor for MFA-interception/token-replay.
- **Network segmentation** to limit lateral movement; restrict/monitor SMB and admin-share usage and east-west traffic.
- Disable unused services/ports; remove/limit unnecessary external remote services.
- Apply the full ACSC AA24-190A mitigation set.

# APT41 — APT Profile

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | People's Republic of China — state-sponsored espionage + financially-motivated cybercrime; contractor front Chengdu 404 |
| Aliases | Wicked Panda, Double Dragon, Barium, Brass Typhoon, Winnti Group, Wicked Spider, Earth Longzhi |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G0096 |
| First seen | 2012 |
| Status | Active |
| Motivation | Espionage (state) + Financial (moonlighting) |

## Overview
APT41 is a prolific China-nexus threat group active since at least 2012 and uniquely characterised by a **dual mandate**: state-directed cyber-espionage carried out in parallel with personal, financially-motivated cybercrime ("moonlighting" off-hours). Mandiant's flagship report titled this duality **"Double Dragon."** The group is one of the most operationally versatile Chinese actors, weaponising newly disclosed vulnerabilities within hours, running software supply-chain compromises, and maintaining a deep custom-malware arsenal alongside commodity tooling.

## Attribution & Origin
- Attributed to the PRC; assessed as state-sponsored espionage running alongside profit-driven hacking by the same individuals.
- Contractor front company: **Chengdu 404 Network Technology Co., Ltd.**, posing as a network-security firm.
- **DOJ September 2020 indictments** (grand-jury indictments Aug 2019 & Aug 2020, Washington D.C.): seven defendants tied to intrusions affecting 100+ organisations worldwide.
  - Five PRC nationals (FBI Cyber's Most Wanted): Zhang Haoran, Tan Dailin, Jiang Lizhi, Qian Chuan, Fu Qiang. Jiang/Qian/Fu were tied to Chengdu 404 and charged with RICO racketeering, CFAA, access-device fraud, aggravated identity theft and money laundering.
  - Two Malaysian nationals (Wong Ong Hua, Ling Yang Ching) charged for the financially-motivated video-game side; arrested in Malaysia.
- Significance: first time members of a Chinese APT faced US federal charges for **both** state espionage and profit-driven hacking.

## Motivation & Objectives
- **State espionage** — IP theft, telecom call/SMS collection, government access; aligned with PRC Five-Year-Plan strategic intelligence requirements.
- **Financial** — video-game virtual-currency manipulation, Monero cryptomining, ransomware attempts, and theft of code-signing certificates.

## Target Profile
Sectors span healthcare, high-tech, telecommunications, software/video games, finance, education, retail, manufacturing, defense, energy, aerospace, biotech/pharma, media, shipping/logistics and government (incl. US state governments).

**APAC / Australia relevance.** APT41 has hit victims across 14+ countries with a heavy APAC footprint (US, Australia, Hong Kong, India, Indonesia, Japan, Malaysia, Pakistan, Singapore, South Korea, Taiwan, Thailand, Vietnam, Chile, Brazil). The group has **specifically targeted telecommunications providers in Australia**, alongside telcos in Chile, India, Indonesia, Malaysia, Pakistan, Singapore, South Korea, Taiwan, Thailand and China (Tibet). The MESSAGETAP SMS/CDR collection operation and broad telco targeting make APT41 directly relevant to Australian and APAC carrier defenders. The Trend Micro-tracked subgroup **Earth Longzhi** and the 2024 **APT41 DUST** campaign (Mandiant/Google) repeatedly struck APAC organisations.

> Honesty note: no ACSC/ASD advisory names "APT41" explicitly; Australian relevance is established via documented Australian telecom targeting (Mandiant) plus general ACSC PRC-APT advisories rather than a named ACSC APT41 callout.

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK
Technique IDs verified against the MITRE ATT&CK G0096 page (and campaigns C0017, C0040).

**Initial Access**
- `T1190` Exploit Public-Facing Application — CVE-2019-19781 (Citrix ADC), CVE-2020-10189 (Zoho ManageEngine), CVE-2021-26855 (ProxyLogon), CVE-2021-44228 (Log4Shell), CVE-2021-44207 (USAHerds).
- `T1566.001` Phishing: Spearphishing Attachment — malicious compiled-HTML (.chm) attachments.
- `T1133` External Remote Services — abused third-party VPN to reach a billing/payment service.

**Execution**
- `T1203` Exploitation for Client Execution — weaponised CVE-2017-0199, CVE-2017-11882, CVE-2015-1641.
- `T1059` Command and Scripting Interpreter — PowerShell and cmd for tasking.

**Persistence**
- `T1505.003` Server Software Component: Web Shell — JScript/ViewState web shells; ANTSWORD/BLUEBEAM in DUST.
- `T1053.005` Scheduled Task — tasks disguised under `\Microsoft\Windows\WDI\...` for DEADEYE persistence.

**Privilege Escalation / Defense Evasion**
- `T1574.001` Hijack Execution Flow: DLL — DLL search-order hijacking/side-loading to launch Winnti and DUSTTRAP.
- `T1027` Obfuscated Files or Information — VMProtect/Themida packing; split DEADEYE/KEYPLUG binaries on disk.
- `T1036.005` Masquerading: Match Legitimate Name/Location — disguised malware as AV software; renamed KEYPLUG extensions.
- `T1542.003` Pre-OS Boot: Bootkit — MBR bootkits for stealth/persistence.

**Credential Access**
- `T1003.001` OS Credential Dumping: LSASS Memory — Mimikatz/Procdump/WCE (also T1003.002 SAM, T1003.003 NTDS via ntdsutil).

**Lateral Movement**
- `T1021.001` Remote Services: RDP — RDP exposed via NATBypass.

**Collection / C2 / Exfiltration**
- `T1090` Proxy — Cloudflare CDN to proxy C2 (C0017); `T1071` Application Layer Protocol — web/DNS C2.
- `T1567` Exfiltration Over Web Service.

**Impact (financial side)**
- `T1496.001` Resource Hijacking: Compute Hijacking — Monero cryptominer deployed in victim environment.

**Resource Development**
- `T1588.003` Obtain Capabilities: Code Signing Certificates — stolen certs to sign DUSTTRAP; maintains a certificate library.

## Tooling & Malware
- **Espionage backdoors/loaders:** Winnti for Windows (S0141), ShadowPad, PlugX, KEYPLUG/KEYPLUG.LINUX (S1051, WSS-over-TLS C2 via Cloudflare), DEADEYE (S1052), DUSTTRAP/DUSTPAN (S1159/S1158), MESSAGETAP (telecom SMS/CDR interception), DEPLOYLOG (Operation CuckooBees).
- **Other named tooling:** GEARSHIFT (keylogger), GOODLUCK (credential theft), WIDETONE (port scanner), CLASSFON (proxy), NATBypass, BadPotato.
- **Commodity / dual-use:** Cobalt Strike (S0154), Mimikatz (S0002), pwdump, PowerSploit, WCE, Procdump, certutil, ntdsutil; packers VMProtect and Themida.

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **2017–2018** — Software supply-chain compromises: trojanised CCleaner (2018), ASUS Live Update "ShadowHammer" (delivered to ~1M users, signed with legit ASUS certs, targeting ~600 MAC addresses); NetSarang also linked.
- **2019** — MESSAGETAP discovered (FireEye/Mandiant): telecom malware siphoning SMS by phone number/IMSI/keyword and stealing call detail records of high-value individuals.
- **Aug 2019 & Aug 2020 (announced Sep 2020)** — DOJ grand-jury indictments; 7 defendants tied to APT41/Chengdu 404; 100+ victims; FBI Most Wanted listings.
- **2020** — "Global Intrusion Campaign Using Multiple Exploits" (FireEye/Mandiant): mass exploitation of Citrix, Cisco, Zoho ManageEngine.
- **May 2021–Feb 2022 (C0017)** — Compromised at least six US state government networks via vulnerable web apps and Log4Shell within hours of disclosure; deployed KEYPLUG.LINUX and DEADEYE; re-compromised two victims via a USAHerds livestock app.
- **May 2022** — Operation CuckooBees (Cybereason): years-long undetected IP-theft campaign vs manufacturing/tech in NA, Europe, Asia; uncovered DEPLOYLOG and new Winnti/ShadowPad variants.
- **2023–2024 (C0040, "APT41 DUST")** — DUSTTRAP/DUSTPAN, DLL side-loading (incl. via AhnLab uninstaller), stolen code-signing certs; victims across shipping/logistics, media, tech, automotive — significant APAC targeting.

## Detection & Hunting
- Hunt DLL search-order hijacking/side-loading: signed EXEs loading unsigned DLLs from writable dirs (Winnti, DUSTTRAP, ShadowPad pattern).
- Alert on `certutil.exe` downloading files and web shells writing to IIS/Exchange dirs (ViewState/JScript, ANTSWORD/BLUEBEAM).
- Monitor KEYPLUG indicators: WebSocket-over-TLS (WSS) C2 and C2 proxied through Cloudflare CDN from servers that should not use it.
- Watch scheduled tasks masquerading under `\Microsoft\Windows\WDI\...` / `\PLA\...` and `SCHTASKS /Change` on legitimate tasks.
- Detect credential-dump tooling (Mimikatz, Procdump on lsass, `reg save SAM/SYSTEM`, `ntdsutil` ntds.dit copies) and MBR/bootkit changes.
- Telecom defenders: monitor anomalous access to HLR/CDR/SMSC databases and unexpected processes reading SMS/CDR stores (MESSAGETAP).
- Code-signing: alert on binaries signed with revoked/unexpected certificates.

## Mitigations
- Patch fast — prioritise internet-facing apps APT41 weaponises within hours (Log4Shell, ProxyLogon, Citrix ADC, Zoho ManageEngine, USAHerds-class apps).
- Enforce MFA; restrict/segment RDP and SMB; limit external remote-service/VPN trust.
- Application allow-listing and EDR tuned to side-loading and LOLBins (certutil, ntdsutil, reg, schtasks).
- LSASS protection (Credential Guard / RunAsPPL); restrict admin shares; rotate/monitor code-signing keys.
- Verify software-update integrity (supply-chain monitoring) given the ASUS/CCleaner/NetSarang history.

## Activity Log
<!-- Append-only -->
- _(seed)_ 2026-06-17 — Profile created.

| Date | Event Type | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| | | | |

## References
- MITRE ATT&CK Group G0096: https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0096/
- MITRE ATT&CK Campaign C0017 (APT41 US state govts): https://attack.mitre.org/campaigns/C0017/
- MITRE ATT&CK Campaign C0040 (APT41 DUST): https://attack.mitre.org/campaigns/C0040/
- DOJ press release (Sept 2020 indictments): https://www.justice.gov/archives/opa/pr/seven-international-cyber-defendants-including-apt41-actors-charged-connection-computer
- FBI Wanted — APT41 Group: https://www.fbi.gov/wanted/cyber/apt-41-group
- Mandiant "APT41: Double Dragon": https://www.mandiant.com/resources/report-apt41-double-dragon-a-dual-espionage-and-cyber-crime-operation
- Mandiant — APT41 Targeting US State Government Networks: https://cloud.google.com/blog/topics/threat-intelligence/apt41-us-state-governments
- Mandiant — APT41 Has Arisen From the DUST: https://cloud.google.com/blog/topics/threat-intelligence/apt41-arisen-from-dust
- Mandiant — MESSAGETAP: https://cloud.google.com/blog/topics/threat-intelligence/messagetap-who-is-reading-your-text-messages/
- Cybereason — Operation CuckooBees: https://www.cybereason.com/blog/operation-cuckoobees-cybereason-uncovers-massive-chinese-intellectual-property-theft-operation
- ACSC / Cyber.gov.au — PRC APT advisory (health sector): https://www.cyber.gov.au/about-us/alerts/advanced-persistent-threat-apt-actors-targeting-australian-health-sector-organisations-and-covid-19-essential-services

# BlueNoroff — APT Profile

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | North Korea — RGB (Lazarus financial subgroup / APT38) |
| Aliases | APT38, Stardust Chollima, Sapphire Sleet, TA444, BeagleBoyz, NICKEL GLADSTONE |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G0082 |
| First seen | 2014 |
| Status | Active (DPRK's primary financial-theft unit) |
| Motivation | Financial (regime revenue); some espionage support |

## Overview
BlueNoroff is the financially motivated North Korean threat actor tracked by MITRE as **APT38 (G0082)** and widely reported under the name BlueNoroff (Kaspersky), Sapphire Sleet (Microsoft), and TA444 (Proofpoint). It operates as the money-making arm of the broader Lazarus umbrella under the RGB. Where Lazarus and Kimsuky pursue mixed espionage/financial goals, BlueNoroff is laser-focused on stealing money: it pioneered the SWIFT bank heists (including the 2016 Bangladesh Bank theft), ATM cash-out schemes (the "FASTCash"/BeagleBoyz operations), and — in recent years — large-scale cryptocurrency theft from exchanges, DeFi platforms, and individual blockchain professionals.

## Attribution & Origin
APT38/BlueNoroff is attributed to North Korea's Reconnaissance General Bureau. The U.S. CISA/FBI/Treasury "HIDDEN COBRA" and "BeagleBoyz" advisories, OFAC sanctions, and vendor reporting (Kaspersky, Mandiant, Microsoft, Proofpoint, Kaspersky's GReAT/Securelist) all attribute its financial operations to the DPRK. It shares code, infrastructure, and tradecraft with the wider Lazarus cluster but is consistently distinguished by its bank/crypto-theft mission.

## Motivation & Objectives
- **Financial (overwhelming):** generate hard currency for the sanctioned DPRK regime via bank fraud, ATM cash-outs, and cryptocurrency theft.
- **Espionage (supporting):** reconnaissance of financial institutions, SWIFT environments, and crypto/Web3 organizations to enable theft.

## Target Profile
BlueNoroff targets the financial ecosystem worldwide: cryptocurrency exchanges, blockchain/Web3 startups, venture capital firms, fintechs, banks, SWIFT-connected institutions, and ATM networks. Its **SnatchCrypto** and **GhostCall** operations heavily target blockchain developers, founders, and C-level/VC executives. **APAC / Australia & financial/crypto relevance is very high:** BlueNoroff has hit financial institutions and crypto firms across the Asia-Pacific, Middle East, and globally. Australian and APAC crypto exchanges, Web3 startups, and the VCs that fund them are direct targets — the group approaches victims via Telegram/LinkedIn with fake investment meetings and fake-Zoom/Microsoft Teams lures, increasingly on **macOS** (executives' machines). Regional fintech and exchange security teams should treat unsolicited "investor call" and meeting-software-update prompts as high-risk indicators.

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK

### Initial Access
- **T1566.003 Spearphishing via Service** — outreach over Telegram/LinkedIn posing as investors, recruiters, or journalists; fake-Zoom and Microsoft Teams meeting lures (GhostCall).
- **T1189 Drive-by Compromise** and **T1204.002 User Execution: Malicious File** — fake meeting-software "updates" and trojanized documents/installers.
- **T1195-class supply chain** — malicious Go/npm packages used to reach developers.

### Execution
- **T1059.002 AppleScript** (macOS execution chains), **T1059.007 JavaScript**, **T1059.001 PowerShell / .003 Windows Command Shell** — modular loaders written in Rust, Go, Objective-C, and scripting languages.
- **T1106 Native API**, **T1129 Shared Modules**, **T1657 Financial Theft** (the defining impact-stage objective).

### Persistence & Privilege Escalation
- **T1546.004 Unix Shell Configuration Modification** (novel macOS persistence via shell profiles/zshenv), **T1543 Create/Modify System Process**, **T1547 Boot/Logon Autostart**, **T1548 Abuse Elevation Control** (macOS osascript privilege prompts).

### Defense Evasion
- **T1553.001 Gatekeeper Bypass** and **T1553 Subvert Trust Controls** (signed/notarized or trust-abusing macOS payloads), **T1027 Obfuscated Files**, **T1140 Deobfuscate/Decode**, **T1112 Modify Registry** (Windows), **T1480 Execution Guardrails**.

### Credential Access & Discovery
- **T1555 Credentials from Password Stores** (keychain, browser, wallet data), **T1003 OS Credential Dumping**, **T1082 System Information Discovery**, **T1057 Process Discovery**, **T1518 Software Discovery** (hunting wallet/crypto apps).

### Collection, C2 & Exfiltration
- **T1113 Screen Capture**, **T1056 Input Capture/keylogging**, **T1115 Clipboard Data** (crypto-address swapping), **T1560 Archive Collected Data**.
- **T1071.001 Web Protocols (HTTPS)**, plus WebSockets and the **Telegram Bot API** for exfiltrating screenshots/keystrokes; **T1105 Ingress Tool Transfer**, **T1571 Non-Standard Port**, **T1041 Exfiltration Over C2 Channel**.

### Impact
- **T1657 Financial Theft** (core objective — fraudulent SWIFT transfers, ATM cash-outs, crypto draining), **T1485 Data Destruction** and **T1486 Data Encrypted for Impact** (anti-forensics/cover after bank heists, e.g., disk-wiping post-SWIFT theft), **T1489 Service Stop**.

## Tooling & Malware
- **SnatchCrypto** toolkit — long-running crypto-theft operation against Web3/blockchain firms.
- **RustBucket** — multi-stage macOS backdoor (AppleScript → Swift/Rust stages) targeting crypto staff.
- **KANDYKORN** — macOS implant delivered via fake crypto trading bots to blockchain engineers.
- **ObjCShellz** — Objective-C macOS remote shell used in the RustBucket campaign chain.
- **GhostCall / GhostHire** — 2025 campaigns: fake-Zoom/Teams executive lures (GhostCall) and developer-recruiting lures (GhostHire) delivering modular Rust/Go malware over HTTPS/WebSockets/Telegram.
- **FASTCash** (BeagleBoyz) — ATM cash-out malware injecting fraudulent responses into bank payment switches.
- Windows backdoors shared with Lazarus (e.g., Hoplight/DOWNDELPH-style implants) for SWIFT-environment operations.

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **2015–2016** — SWIFT bank-heist operations; **Bangladesh Bank** theft (~$81M, 2016) and attempts against other banks.
- **2018** — CISA/FBI **FASTCash** (HIDDEN COBRA) advisories on ATM cash-out attacks; **BeagleBoyz** bank-targeting reporting.
- **2017–2022** — **SnatchCrypto** campaigns against crypto startups and Web3 firms worldwide (Kaspersky).
- **2023** — **RustBucket**, **KANDYKORN**, and **ObjCShellz** macOS campaigns target crypto/blockchain engineers and exchanges.
- **2024** — Continued macOS focus; supply-chain attacks using malicious Go packages; pivot to **Microsoft Teams** impersonation for malware delivery (Microsoft/Sapphire Sleet reporting).
- **Late 2025** — **GhostCall & GhostHire** campaigns (Kaspersky Securelist): fake-Zoom/Teams calls and AI deepfakes target macOS devices of tech/VC executives; 80+ typosquatted C2 domains; HTTPS/WebSocket/Telegram exfiltration.

## Detection & Hunting
- macOS hunting: alert on **osascript/AppleScript** spawning network activity, modifications to shell config files (`.zshenv`, `.zprofile`), and **Gatekeeper bypass** / unsigned binary execution from user directories.
- Detect fake meeting-software: "Zoom/Teams update" prompts, look-alike meeting domains, and binaries masquerading as conferencing clients.
- Monitor for **clipboard-monitoring/crypto-address swapping** and reads of wallet/keychain/browser-credential stores.
- Network: HTTPS to newly-registered typosquatted domains, **WebSocket** C2, and **Telegram Bot API** traffic from endpoints; flag exfil of screenshots/keystrokes.
- In financial environments: monitor SWIFT/payment-switch hosts for anomalous transactions and post-theft wiping (T1485) behavior.
- Watch developer machines for malicious Go/npm packages and "investment/interview" lure files.

## Mitigations
- macOS hardening: enforce Gatekeeper/notarization, restrict osascript, monitor shell-config integrity, deploy macOS-capable EDR.
- Phishing-resistant **FIDO2 MFA**; verify meeting invitations and software updates out-of-band; block conferencing-app spoofing domains.
- Lock down crypto-operations and treasury workflows; hardware wallets and multi-party approval for transfers; out-of-band confirmation.
- Package-management controls (private registries, scanning, pinning) to counter malicious Go/npm packages.
- Network egress filtering; alert on WebSocket/Telegram C2 and typosquatted-domain resolution.
- Targeted awareness for executives, founders, VCs, and blockchain developers on fake-investor and fake-recruiter social engineering.
