# Scattered Spider — APT Profile

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | Financially-motivated cybercrime collective (English-speaking; US/UK members) |
| Aliases | UNC3944, Octo Tempest, Roasted 0ktapus, Scatter Swine, Storm-0875, Muddled Libra |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G1015 |
| First seen | 2022 |
| Status | Active |
| Motivation | Financial / extortion |

## Overview
Scattered Spider (MITRE **G1015**) is a large, loosely affiliated, **financially motivated** cybercrime collective active since at least 2022. Unlike the China-nexus espionage groups, it is **native-English-speaking** (members largely in the US and UK, linked to the broader "Com" / "Comm" online ecosystem) and is defined by **best-in-class social engineering** rather than custom malware. The group specialises in help-desk impersonation, MFA-fatigue push bombing, and SIM-swap attacks to seize identities, then pivots to data theft, extortion, and ransomware deployment.

## Attribution & Origin
Scattered Spider is **not state-sponsored** — it is a criminal collective. It is tracked as UNC3944 (Mandiant/Google), Octo Tempest (Microsoft), Roasted 0ktapus/Oktapus (Group-IB, after the 0ktapus phishing kit), Scatter Swine (Okta), Storm-0875, and Muddled Libra (Palo Alto Unit 42). Members are predominantly young, English-speaking individuals; multiple arrests have occurred in the US and UK. CISA/FBI issued joint advisory **AA23-320A** (Nov 16, 2023; updated July 29, 2025) detailing its TTPs.

## Motivation & Objectives
Purely **financial**: monetisation via data-theft extortion and ransomware. The group has affiliated with ransomware-as-a-service operations including **ALPHV/BlackCat** and later **DragonForce**, deploying encryptors after exfiltrating data for double extortion. Targets are chosen for leverage and payout potential.

## Target Profile
Primarily **US and UK** organisations, with documented activity against **Canada, Australia, and Western Europe**. Early waves hit telecommunications and BPO firms (for SIM-swap and downstream access); later campaigns devastated **gaming/casino & hospitality** (notably the 2023 MGM Resorts and Caesars Entertainment intrusions), and expanded into technology, financial services, retail, insurance, and cloud/SaaS providers.

**APAC / Australia note:** While its centre of gravity is the English-speaking US/UK market, Scattered Spider has targeted **Australian** organisations, and the **ACSC (cyber.gov.au) published a dedicated Scattered Spider advisory** mirroring the CISA/FBI guidance — a clear signal of relevance to Australian enterprises, particularly those with large help-desk/BPO operations or consumer-facing identity flows.

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK
**Initial Access — Social Engineering (core strength)**
- T1566.004 Spearphishing Voice (vishing) — impersonating IT/help-desk staff to drive credential/MFA resets.
- T1660 Phishing (smishing) — SMS lures with org-specific look-alike domains (0ktapus-style kits).
- **SIM swapping** — hijacking victim phone numbers to intercept SMS OTP/MFA (mapped under credential/MFA compromise).

**Credential Access & MFA Bypass**
- T1621 Multi-Factor Authentication Request Generation — MFA fatigue / push bombing.
- T1556.006 Modify Authentication Process: MFA — registering attacker-controlled MFA tokens.
- T1606 Forge Web Credentials — session/token abuse.

**Persistence & Privilege**
- T1136 Create Account — creating their own user/admin accounts.
- T1098.005 Account Manipulation: Device Registration — enrolling attacker devices.
- T1078 Valid Accounts — using stolen/legitimate credentials.

**Execution & Defense Evasion — Living off legitimate tools**
- T1219 Remote Access Software — AnyDesk, TeamViewer, ScreenConnect, Splashtop, Splashtop pushed onto victims.

**Trusted Relationship**
- T1199 Trusted Relationship — compromising third-party/SaaS providers to reach multiple downstream targets.

**Impact & Exfiltration**
- T1567 Exfiltration Over Web Service — data theft to attacker cloud/storage.
- T1486 Data Encrypted for Impact — ALPHV/BlackCat then DragonForce ransomware.

## Tooling & Malware
Scattered Spider is a **"living-off-the-land" / legitimate-tooling** actor, not a malware developer:
- **Remote access:** AnyDesk, TeamViewer, ScreenConnect (ConnectWise), Splashtop, Pulseway, ngrok tunnelling.
- **Credential/info-stealers:** RACCOON Stealer, VIDAR Stealer, Mimikatz.
- **RATs:** RattyRAT (Java RAT) in some intrusions.
- **Ransomware (affiliate):** ALPHV/BlackCat (earlier), **DragonForce** (later).
- **Phishing kits:** 0ktapus / EIGHTBAIT-style credential-harvest kits with org-branded domains.

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **2022** — Emergence; 0ktapus SMS phishing campaign compromises 100+ orgs (telecom/BPO focus) for SIM-swap and downstream access.
- **2023** — High-profile help-desk-driven intrusions; **MGM Resorts** and **Caesars Entertainment** (Sept 2023) — vishing the help desk, ALPHV/BlackCat deployment, major operational/financial impact.
- **Nov 16, 2023** — CISA/FBI joint advisory **AA23-320A** published.
- **2024** — Multiple arrests (US/UK); shift in RaaS affiliation; continued SaaS/cloud and finance targeting.
- **Jul 29, 2025** — AA23-320A advisory updated with expanded TTPs and **DragonForce** ransomware association.
- **2025–2026** — Sustained help-desk/identity-centric extortion across retail, insurance, aviation, and tech sectors.

## Detection & Hunting
- **Help-desk abuse:** alert on credential/MFA reset requests, especially out-of-band or for privileged accounts; correlate with inbound calls. Enforce and log identity-verification challenges.
- **MFA anomalies:** new MFA device/token registrations (T1556.006, T1098.005), bursts of push prompts (T1621), logins immediately following resets.
- **SIM-swap indicators:** sudden MFA-delivery changes, carrier-side number ports, OTP delivery to new devices.
- **Remote access tools:** detect installation/execution of AnyDesk, ScreenConnect, TeamViewer, Splashtop, ngrok where not sanctioned (T1219).
- **New account creation** and privilege escalation in IdP (Okta/Entra ID) audit logs (T1136).
- **Impossible-travel / atypical geo** logins on cloud/SaaS; session-token reuse anomalies.

## Mitigations
- **Phishing-resistant MFA** (FIDO2/WebAuthn hardware keys) — removes push-bombing and OTP-interception vectors.
- **Harden help-desk identity verification** — out-of-band, multi-factor proof before any password/MFA reset; supervisor approval for privileged resets.
- **Restrict / allow-list remote access software**; block unsanctioned RMM via application control.
- **Limit SIM-swap exposure** — remove SMS as an MFA factor; carrier port-out PINs.
- **Tighten IdP controls** — monitor MFA enrolment, conditional access, device registration; least privilege for admins (PAM).
- **Third-party/SaaS risk management** to counter trusted-relationship abuse (T1199).
- **Immutable/offline backups** and rapid IR plan for ransomware (T1486).

## Activity Log
<!-- Append-only -->
- _(seed)_ 2026-06-17 — Profile created.

## Trend Tracking
| Date | Event Type | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| | | | |

## References
- https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G1015/
- https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa23-320a
- https://www.cisa.gov/sites/default/files/2023-11/aa23-320a_scattered_spider_0.pdf
- https://www.ic3.gov/CSA/2025/250729.pdf
- https://www.cyber.gov.au/about-us/view-all-content/alerts-and-advisories/scattered-spider
- https://attack.mitre.org/techniques/T1566/004/
- https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Scattered_Spider

# Turla — APT Profile

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | Russia — FSB Center 16 |
| Aliases | Snake, Uroburos, Venomous Bear, Secret Blizzard, Waterbug, KRYPTON |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G0010 |
| First seen | 2004 |
| Status | Active |
| Motivation | Espionage |

## Overview
Turla is a long-running Russian state cyber-espionage group attributed to a unit within Center 16 of the FSB. Active since at least 2004 (with tooling lineage tracing to ~2003), it is one of the most technically sophisticated and stealthy intrusion sets ever observed, distinguished by extreme patience, multi-year dwell times, highly selective targeting of high-value victims, and aggressive anti-attribution tradecraft.

Turla pioneered unusual techniques including satellite-based C2 (hijacking one-way downstream satellite IPs to anonymize command-and-control), hijacking other threat actors' infrastructure and tooling (Iranian OilRig in 2019; Pakistani Storm-0156 and the Amadey crimeware botnet in 2024–25), and operating at the ISP level for adversary-in-the-middle attacks (ApolloShadow, 2025). It is publicly linked to the 2008 Agent.btz worm that breached US CENTCOM.

The group is not financially motivated and runs no destructive or ransomware operations; its mission is long-term intelligence collection on behalf of the Russian state. Its flagship Snake implant was disrupted by the FBI's Operation MEDUSA in May 2023.

## Attribution & Origin
- **Country / sponsor:** Russia — FSB, specifically a unit within Center 16 (the FSB's signals-intelligence directorate). The US Government attributes Snake malware operations directly to "a known unit within Center 16 of the FSB."
- **Operation MEDUSA (May 9, 2023):** Court-authorized FBI operation that disrupted the global Snake P2P network using a purpose-built tool named PERSEUS, which forced Snake implants to overwrite their own vital components. Announced by DOJ (EDNY) with joint advisory AA23-129A.
- **NSA/NCSC joint advisory (Oct 21, 2019):** Exposed Turla's hijacking of Iranian APT34/OilRig infrastructure.
- **Sanctions note:** No standalone OFAC designation is publicly tied specifically to the "Turla/Center 16" label; the primary public countermeasures are the legal/technical disruption (Operation MEDUSA, May 2023) plus repeated multinational attribution.

## Motivation & Objectives
Long-term cyber-espionage and intelligence collection on behalf of the Russian state. Hallmarks: extreme stealth, patience, and operational longevity (years of dwell time), highly selective targeting of high-value victims, and aggressive anti-attribution tradecraft (satellite C2, hijacking other actors' infrastructure/tools). Not financially motivated; no destructive/ransomware mission.

## Target Profile
**Sectors:** Government, ministries of foreign affairs, embassies/diplomatic missions, military and defense contractors, intelligence agencies, research/education, pharmaceutical, and journalists. Victims span 50+ countries.

**Regions:** Primarily NATO members, Europe, and former Soviet states, but the Snake infrastructure footprint is global.

**APAC / Australia targeting:** Australia is directly implicated.
- The May 2023 joint Snake advisory (CISA AA23-129A) states Snake infrastructure was found across North America, South America, Europe, Africa, Asia, and Australia.
- ACSC/ASD co-sealed the Snake advisory. Australia's ACSC (part of ASD) was a co-authoring agency on the 2023 Snake/Operation MEDUSA joint advisory alongside FBI, NSA, CISA, CNMF, Canada's CCCS/CSE, and New Zealand's NCSC — confirming Australian government concern about Turla targeting in the region.

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK

### Initial Access
- **T1189 – Drive-by Compromise:** Infects victims via watering holes (compromised diplomatic/government-relevant websites).
- **T1566 / T1204.001 – Phishing → User Execution (Malicious Link):** Spearphishing with links to deliver first-stage droppers.

### Execution
- **T1059.001 / .003 / .005 / .006 / .007 – Command & Scripting Interpreter:** Uses PowerShell, cmd.exe, VBScript, IronPython (IronNetInjector), and multiple JavaScript backdoors.

### Persistence
- **T1547.004 – Boot/Logon Autostart (Winlogon Helper DLL):** Adds a Shell value under HKCU\...\Winlogon.
- **T1546.003 / .013 – Event Triggered Execution:** WMI event subscriptions and malicious PowerShell profiles for persistence.

### Privilege Escalation
- **T1068 – Exploitation for Privilege Escalation:** Exploited the VBoxDrv.sys (VirtualBox driver) vulnerability for kernel-mode privileges (BYOVD).
- **T1134.002 – Access Token Manipulation (Create Process with Token):** RPC backdoors impersonate/steal tokens before running commands.

### Defense Evasion
- **T1027.011 – Obfuscated Files/Info (Fileless Storage):** Stores encrypted/encoded payloads in the Registry.
- **T1112 – Modify Registry:** Stores payloads in registry values; uses Reg (S0075).
- **T1055 / .001 – Process Injection / Reflective DLL Injection:** Reflective PE/PowerShell injection (PowerSploit, Metasploit).

### Credential Access
- **T1555.004 – Credentials from Windows Credential Manager.**
- **T1110 – Brute Force:** net use against systems with a predefined password list.
- **T1588.002 – Obtain Capabilities (Tool):** Customizes Mimikatz (S0002).

### Discovery
- **T1087.002 – Account Discovery (Domain):** net user /domain.
- **T1082 – System Information Discovery:** systeminfo, set.
- **T1615 – Group Policy Discovery:** gpresult.
- *(Plus T1007 tasklist /svc, T1124 net time, T1069.002 net group "Domain Admins" /domain.)*

### Lateral Movement
- **T1021.002 – Remote Services (SMB/Admin Shares):** net use to connect to lateral systems.
- **T1570 – Lateral Tool Transfer:** RPC backdoors move files across the internal network.

### Collection
- **T1560.001 – Archive Collected Data (via Utility):** RARs USB-drive data before exfil.
- **T1025 – Data from Removable Media** / **T1005 – Data from Local System.**

### Command and Control
- **T1102 / .002 – Web Service / Bidirectional Communication:** Abuses Pastebin, Dropbox, GitHub, and Google Apps Script as C2.
- **T1090 / .001 – Proxy / Internal Proxy:** Compromises internal hosts to relay traffic to C2 (also UPnP RPC proxies).
- **T1584.006 – Compromise Infrastructure (Web Services):** Frequently hijacks compromised WordPress sites for C2.
- **T1071.003 – Application Layer Protocol (Mail):** Backdoors (e.g., LightNeuron) receive commands via email attachments.

### Exfiltration
- **T1583.006 – Acquire Infrastructure: Web Services:** Document exfiltration via Dropbox/GitHub web accounts.

> **Signature tradecraft to flag:** satellite-based C2 ("satlink hijacking," since ~2007 per Kaspersky — hijacks one-way downstream satellite IPs to anonymize C2); hijacking other actors' infrastructure (Iranian OilRig/APT34 in 2019; Pakistani Storm-0156 and the Amadey MaaS botnet in 2024–25); and watering-hole compromises (T1189).

## Tooling & Malware
- **Snake / Uroburos (S0022):** Flagship modular kernel-mode rootkit/espionage platform; cross-platform (Windows/Linux/macOS).
- **ComRAT (S0126):** Second-stage implant descended from Agent.btz; latest versions use the Gmail web UI for C2.
- **Kazuar (S0265):** Multi-platform .NET espionage backdoor with an extensive API; updated version deployed in Ukraine 2024.
- **Carbon (S0335):** Modular second-stage backdoor framework (successor to earlier Uroburos components).
- **Mosquito:** JavaScript/installer backdoor delivered via fake Flash installers; later pivoted toward generic tools (Metasploit).
- **LightNeuron:** First known Microsoft Exchange Transport Agent backdoor; reads/modifies/blocks all mail, controlled via steganographic PDF/JPG email attachments (active since ~2014).
- **Crutch (S0538):** Backdoor using Dropbox for exfiltration of documents/USB data.
- **TinyTurla (S0668):** Lightweight backup/second-chance backdoor (service DLL); TinyTurla-NG variant later seen vs. Ukraine.
- **Penquin / Penquin Turla (S0587):** Linux backdoor/RAT (BPF-based covert wake-up), active since ~2014.
- **Gazer (S0168):** Second-stage backdoor used against embassies/consulates from ~2016.
- **Tavdig (Wipbot/Epic):** Loader/backdoor used to chain into Kazuar in the 2024 Amadey campaign.
- **ApolloShadow (2025):** Newest custom malware; installs a rogue root certificate and enables TLS interception in the ISP-level AiTM embassy campaign.
- **Customized commodity tools:** Mimikatz, Metasploit/Meterpreter, PowerSploit.

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **2008** — Agent.btz: USB worm breaches US CENTCOM ("worst breach of US military computers in history"); triggered Operation Buckshot Yankee and the creation of US Cyber Command. Later code-linked to Turla.
- **2007–ongoing** — Satellite C2 hijacking: Kaspersky (2015) exposed Turla hiding C2 behind hijacked satellite downlink IPs.
- **2014** — RUAG (Switzerland): long-running intrusion into a Swiss state-owned defense firm; ~23 GB exfiltrated (disclosed 2016).
- **~2014–2019** — LightNeuron: Exchange Transport-Agent backdoor vs. an Eastern-European MFA and a Middle East diplomatic body (ESET, May 2019).
- **2019** — OilRig/APT34 infrastructure hijack: NSA/NCSC joint advisory (Oct 2019) — Turla took over Iranian APT34 tools and C2, scanning across 35+ countries.
- **2020–2021** — ComRAT v4 / Crutch / TinyTurla: continued diplomatic and government targeting; novel cloud-service C2.
- **May 9, 2023** — Operation MEDUSA / Snake takedown: FBI's PERSEUS tool disables the global Snake P2P network; joint advisory AA23-129A co-sealed by Five Eyes (incl. ACSC/ASD).
- **Nov 2022–2024** — Storm-0156 piggybacking: Microsoft (Dec 2024) — Secret Blizzard compromised Pakistan-based Storm-0156's C2 to spy on South Asian government/military targets.
- **Mar–Apr 2024** — Amadey MaaS abuse (Ukraine): commandeered the Amadey crimeware botnet to drop Tavdig → updated Kazuar onto Ukrainian military devices.
- **Feb–Jul 2025** — ApolloShadow / ISP-level AiTM (embassies in Moscow): Microsoft (Jul 31, 2025) — first confirmed Turla capability to operate at the ISP level, hijacking Windows captive-portal/connectivity checks to push a rogue root cert and intercept TLS against foreign diplomats in Russia.

## Detection & Hunting
- **Process/command-line telemetry:** Hunt the recon cluster: systeminfo, gpresult, tasklist /svc, net user /domain, net group "Domain Admins" /domain, net accounts /domain, fsutil fsinfo drives, net time, tracert — especially in rapid succession from one host (classic Turla check-in survey).
- **Registry:** Watch for new Shell values under HKCU\...\Winlogon; large/encoded blobs written to registry (fileless payload storage, T1027.011).
- **Persistence:** Audit WMI event consumers/filters and modifications to PowerShell profile files.
- **Exchange:** Inventory and validate all Transport Agents (LightNeuron); alert on unsigned/unexpected agents and outbound mail anomalies.
- **Network/C2:** Anomalous use of Pastebin/Dropbox/GitHub/Google Apps Script by server or service accounts; outbound to compromised WordPress sites; odd traffic to one-way satellite IP ranges; for the 2025 vector, monitor captive-portal redirects, unexpected new root certificates, firewall-rule changes, and new hidden local admin accounts.
- **Drivers:** Detect VBoxDrv.sys load on systems without VirtualBox (BYOVD / T1068).
- **Credential tooling:** Mimikatz/Meterpreter behavioral detections; reflective DLL/PE injection.
- **IOCs/signatures:** Use CISA AA23-129A (Snake host-file artifacts, named-pipe/HTTP signatures, Snort/YARA) and ESET/Kaspersky/Microsoft hashes for ComRAT, Kazuar, Crutch, TinyTurla, Penquin, ApolloShadow. Treat IOCs as supplementary — Turla rotates infra heavily; prioritize behavior.

## Mitigations
- Patch aggressively (especially internet-facing servers and Exchange); restrict/monitor driver loading and enable HVCI/driver block-lists to counter BYOVD.
- Eliminate shared local-admin passwords (LAPS) — Turla abuses identical local creds for lateral movement.
- Enforce MFA, least privilege, and tiered admin; restrict outbound access from servers to cloud file/code services used as C2.
- Network segmentation and egress filtering; inspect/limit anomalous satellite or captive-portal traffic.
- Constrain PowerShell (Constrained Language Mode, logging, AMSI); enable script-block + module logging.
- Harden Exchange: audit Transport Agents; deploy EDR on mail servers.
- Validate the root-certificate store and alert on new trusted roots (counters the 2025 TLS-interception vector).
- Apply mitigations in CISA AA23-129A; use it for retro-hunting Snake.

## Activity Log
<!-- Append-only: future intel sweeps add dated entries below -->
- _(seed)_ 2026-06-17 — Profile created.

## Trend Tracking
| Date | Event Type | Description | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| | | | |

## References
- MITRE ATT&CK G0010: https://attack.mitre.org/groups/G0010/
- CISA AA23-129A (Hunting Russian "Snake" Malware): https://www.cisa.gov/news-events/cybersecurity-advisories/aa23-129a
- Snake CSA PDF: https://media.defense.gov/2023/May/09/2003218554/-1/-1/1/JOINT_CSA_HUNTING_RU_INTEL_SNAKE_MALWARE_20230509.PDF
- US DOJ — Operation MEDUSA / Snake disruption: https://www.justice.gov/usao-edny/pr/justice-department-announces-court-authorized-disruption-snake-malware-network
- NSA/NCSC 2019 advisory (Iranian infra hijack): https://www.ncsc.gov.uk/news/turla-group-exploits-iran-apt-to-expand-coverage-of-victims
- NCSC UK Snake exposure (lists ACSC as partner): https://www.ncsc.gov.uk/news/uk-and-allies-expose-snake-malware-threat-from-russian-cyber-actors
- ACSC / ASD (Australia): https://www.cyber.gov.au/
- Microsoft — Secret Blizzard / Storm-0156 (Dec 2024): https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2024/12/04/frequent-freeloader-part-i-secret-blizzard-compromising-storm-0156-infrastructure-for-espionage/
- Microsoft — ApolloShadow / ISP-level AiTM (Jul 2025): https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2025/07/31/frozen-in-transit-secret-blizzards-aitm-campaign-against-diplomats/
- ESET — LightNeuron whitepaper: https://web-assets.esetstatic.com/wls/2019/05/ESET-LightNeuron.pdf
- Kaspersky — Satellite Turla: https://securelist.com/satellite-turla-apt-command-and-control-in-the-sky/72081/

# Volt Typhoon — APT Profile

## Snapshot
| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Attribution | PRC state-sponsored |
| Aliases | BRONZE SILHOUETTE, Vanguard Panda, DEV-0391, UNC3236, Voltzite, Insidious Taurus, DazedToad |
| MITRE ATT&CK ID | G1017 |
| First seen | 2021 |
| Status | Active |
| Motivation | Espionage + Pre-positioning for sabotage/disruption |

## Overview
Volt Typhoon is a PRC state-sponsored actor and the **canonical living-off-the-land (LOTL) threat group**. Active since at least mid-2021, it focuses on **pre-positioning** within US and allied critical-infrastructure IT networks to enable disruptive or destructive cyberattacks during a future major crisis or conflict (e.g. an Indo-Pacific / Taiwan contingency), rather than near-term data theft. It uses minimal or no custom malware on victim IT networks, abusing built-in Windows tools to blend with normal admin activity. Associated edge-device botnet activity is tracked as MITRE Campaign **C0035 (KV Botnet)**.

## Attribution & Origin
PRC state-sponsored / state-directed. The Feb 2024 advisory assesses the actors are **pre-positioning to be able to launch destructive or disruptive attacks against US critical infrastructure in the event of a major crisis or conflict with the United States** — a strategic shift from traditional espionage.

## Motivation & Objectives
A blend of espionage/information-gathering and — primarily — **pre-positioning for sabotage/disruption**: establishing long-dwell persistent access (in some cases ~5 years) for potential future destructive operations against critical infrastructure.

## Target Profile
Sectors: US critical infrastructure — **communications, energy (power), water/wastewater, transportation** — plus manufacturing, maritime, government, IT, education and construction. Regions: continental US and territories, with notable early targeting of **Guam** (a key US Indo-Pacific military hub).

**APAC / Australia relevance.**
- Microsoft (24 May 2023) first publicly named the actor and detailed Guam/US critical-infrastructure targeting via LOTL.
- CISA/NSA/FBI joint advisory **AA24-038A (7 Feb 2024)** confirmed persistent access (some ~5 years) across multiple critical-infrastructure sectors. It was **co-sealed by the full Five Eyes: US (CISA, NSA, FBI), Australia (ASD's ACSC), Canada (CCCS), UK (NCSC-UK), New Zealand (NCSC-NZ).**
- The earlier advisory **AA23-144A** was also co-authored with **ACSC**. The Five Eyes co-sealing makes the guidance directly relevant to Australian and broader APAC critical-infrastructure operators (e.g. under Australia's SOCI Act regime).

## TTPs — MITRE ATT&CK
Technique IDs verified against the MITRE ATT&CK G1017 page. **LOTL is the defining characteristic** — minimal malware, heavy abuse of native binaries.

**Initial Access**
- `T1190` Exploit Public-Facing Application — exploited internet-facing appliances (e.g. Fortinet/edge devices).
- `T1133` External Remote Services — leveraged externally-facing remote services.

**Execution (LOTL)**
- `T1059.001` Command & Scripting Interpreter: PowerShell — hands-on-keyboard discovery/execution.
- `T1059.003` Windows Command Shell — cmd.exe for discovery and operations.
- `T1047` Windows Management Instrumentation — WMI/wmic for remote execution and discovery.

**Credential Access**
- `T1003.003` OS Credential Dumping: NTDS — used `ntdsutil` to create install media containing AD usernames and password hashes from ntds.dit.
- `T1003.001` OS Credential Dumping: LSASS Memory — dumped LSASS for credentials.
- `T1552.004` Unsecured Credentials: Private Keys — read Chrome Local State AES key to decrypt stored passwords.

**Defense Evasion (LOTL)**
- `T1070.007` Indicator Removal: Clear Network Connection History — edited/inspected server logs to remove their IPs.
- `T1036.005` Masquerading: Match Legitimate Name/Location — renamed FRP/Earthworm binaries (e.g. cisco_up.exe, vm3dservice.exe, WmiPreSV.exe).

**Discovery**
- `T1018` Remote System Discovery — enumerated other hosts on the network.
- `T1016` System Network Configuration Discovery — mapped topology (e.g. via ping).

**Lateral Movement / Persistence**
- `T1078.002` Valid Accounts: Domain Accounts — reused compromised domain credentials.
- `T1021.001` Remote Services: RDP — RDP with valid accounts for lateral movement.

**Command & Control (LOTL)**
- `T1090.001 / T1090.003` Proxy: Internal Proxy (`netsh interface portproxy`) and Multi-hop Proxy — proxied traffic via netsh and through compromised SOHO devices.

**Resource Development**
- `T1584.005` Compromise Infrastructure: Botnet — Cisco/NETGEAR EOL SOHO routers running KV Botnet malware (C0035).
- `T1584.008` Compromise Infrastructure: Network Devices — compromised SOHO edge devices to relay/obfuscate traffic.

## Tooling & Malware
Volt Typhoon relies on **built-in, legitimate tools** rather than custom implants:
- **Native / LOLBins:** `netsh` (notably `netsh interface portproxy`), `wmic`/WMI, PowerShell, the Windows command shell, `ntdsutil` (dump ntds.dit), `ping`, `net` (group/localgroup), `dnscmd`.
- **Credential access:** Mimikatz and direct LSASS access; theft of ntds.dit plus SYSTEM/SECURITY registry hives; harvesting of browser-stored credentials and the Chrome Local State AES key.
- **Off-the-shelf tools (often renamed):** Fast Reverse Proxy (FRP) and Earthworm for tunneling/proxying.
- **Infrastructure/obfuscation:** compromised SOHO routers and edge devices (EOL Cisco and NETGEAR) implanted with **KV Botnet** malware (C0035) to proxy/relay traffic and conceal PRC origin; also compromised PRTG servers and VPS/multi-hop proxies for C2.

## Notable Campaigns & Timeline
- **24 May 2023** — Microsoft publishes "Volt Typhoon targets US critical infrastructure with living-off-the-land techniques," first public naming and Guam/critical-infrastructure disclosure.
- **24 May 2023** — CISA/NSA/FBI + Five Eyes (incl. ACSC) release joint advisory **AA23-144A** ("PRC State-Sponsored Cyber Actor Living off the Land to Evade Detection").
- **Dec 2023 (court authorisation) / Jan 2024** — FBI/DOJ court-authorised operation to disrupt the **KV Botnet** of compromised US SOHO routers used by Volt Typhoon.
- **31 Jan 2024** — FBI Director Christopher Wray testifies to the House Select Committee on the CCP, confirming the KV Botnet takedown and warning of pre-positioning to "wreak havoc" on US critical infrastructure.
- **7 Feb 2024** — CISA/NSA/FBI + Five Eyes release joint advisory **AA24-038A**, confirming long-dwell persistent access and pre-positioning for disruptive/destructive attacks; supplemental LOTL guidance and MAR-10448362-1.v1 (AR24-038A).

## Detection & Hunting
LOTL is the core challenge — minimal malware artifacts mean signature/IOC detection is largely ineffective. Shift to behavioral analytics:
- Baseline normal admin behavior and alert on deviations.
- Hunt suspicious native-tool use: `ntdsutil.exe`/`vssadmin` on domain controllers; `netsh interface portproxy` additions; anomalous `wmic`/PowerShell command lines; `net group`/`net localgroup` recon bursts.
- Monitor for ntds.dit, SYSTEM and SECURITY hive copies written to `C:\Windows\Temp\`, or files with mismatched extensions (e.g. `.gif` appended to ntds.dit).
- Watch for unusual authentication from valid/domain accounts (off-hours, new source hosts, impossible travel) and unexpected RDP/lateral movement.
- Inspect edge/SOHO devices for proxy/relay behavior; assume log deletion (`T1070`).
- Enable, centralise and retain comprehensive logging (PowerShell script-block + module logging, command-line process auditing, Security and DC logs) — long retention is critical given multi-year dwell and local log clearing.

## Mitigations
- Patch and **replace end-of-life / internet-facing edge devices** (routers, VPNs, firewalls); apply vendor updates to public-facing apps promptly.
- Enforce **phishing-resistant MFA**, especially for remote access and administrative/domain accounts; enforce least privilege and rotate credentials after suspected exposure.
- Harden domain controllers; restrict and monitor `netsh portproxy` usage; segment networks and restrict lateral movement (RDP, SMB).
- Secure and centralise logs off-host with extended retention; protect against log clearing.

# CVE-2026-34197 — Apache ActiveMQ Classic RCE via Jolokia API

✅ **Verification:** Verified — 3 independent sources

**Date**: 2026-04-08
**Tags**: #cve #rce #apache #activemq #jolokia #jmx #critical #ai-discovered #horizon3
**Source**: X (@Horizon3Attack) — [https://x.com/Horizon3Attack/status/2041614428752286134](https://x.com/Horizon3Attack/status/2041614428752286134)
**CVE**: CVE-2026-34197
**Severity**: Critical
**Component**: Apache ActiveMQ Classic — web console (Jolokia API endpoint)
**Discovered by**: Horizon3 Attack Team using **Claude AI**

---

## Summary

A **remote code execution vulnerability** in the Apache ActiveMQ Classic web console, exploitable via the **Jolokia API**. The vulnerability is:
- Exploitable with **default credentials** on unmodified deployments
- Exploitable **completely unauthenticated** on certain configurations
- Discovered through AI-assisted vulnerability research using **Claude**

---

## Technical Details

### Attack Vector
The Jolokia API (`/api/jolokia`) provides a JMX-over-HTTP interface to the ActiveMQ broker. This endpoint, when accessible:
1. Allows invocation of arbitrary MBean operations via JSON HTTP requests
2. With default/weak credentials (admin:admin), attacker can invoke `exec` MBean methods
3. Certain non-default configurations expose Jolokia without authentication entirely

### Exploitation
- Target: ActiveMQ Classic web console, typically on port 8161
- Endpoint: `/api/jolokia` or `/jolokia`
- Method: POST request invoking ClassLoader or thread class loading MBean to load remote code
- Impact: Full RCE as the ActiveMQ service account

---

## Affected Configurations

- ActiveMQ Classic with Jolokia enabled (default in many versions)
- Default credentials not changed (admin:admin)
- OR Jolokia endpoint exposed without authentication

---

## Exploitation Indicators

- POST requests to `/api/jolokia` or `/jolokia` with `exec` or `read` operations
- Class loading from external URLs in ActiveMQ logs
- Unusual outbound connections from ActiveMQ service process
- ActiveMQ spawning child processes (shell, java -jar, curl, wget)

---

## Remediation

1. Apply Apache ActiveMQ patch for CVE-2026-34197 immediately
2. **Change default credentials** (admin:admin) if not already done
3. Restrict access to the web console and Jolokia endpoint (firewall, VPN)
4. If Jolokia is not needed, **disable it** in `activemq.xml`
5. Monitor ActiveMQ for unusual JMX/MBean invocations

---

## AI Discovery Note

> This CVE was discovered by Horizon3's Attack Team **using Claude AI** to analyze the ActiveMQ Classic codebase and API surface. This is another data point that AI-assisted vuln research is producing real, critical findings.

See also: [[ai-bugbounty-autonomous-hacking-guide]], [[ai-bugbounty-claude-zeroday-ghost-cms]]

# CVE-2026-46316 — Linux Kernel KVM Guest-to-Host Escape (arm64)

⚠️ **Verification:** Unverified — 1 independent source (single X post; PoC reported public)

## Summary
A **critical Linux kernel vulnerability** (**CVE-2026-46316**) enables a **guest-to-host escape** in **KVM environments on arm64 systems**. A proof-of-concept (PoC) exploit has been publicly released. Because the flaw lives in the in-kernel KVM implementation (not user-space components like QEMU), successful exploitation results in **direct compromise of the host kernel** rather than a confined user-space process — making it especially severe for multi-tenant / cloud arm64 hosts.

## Details
- **CVE:** CVE-2026-46316
- **Affected:** In-kernel KVM implementation on **arm64** (not QEMU/user-space)
- **Root cause:** Race condition in the **vGIC-ITS (Interrupt Translation Service) emulation** within KVM on arm64
- **Impact:** Guest VM → host kernel escape (full host compromise)
- **Discoverer:** Security researcher **Hyunwoo Kim (V4bel)**
- **PoC:** Publicly released (per source)

## Why It Matters
- Direct host-kernel compromise breaks the core VM isolation boundary
- arm64 server/cloud fleets (increasingly common) are exposed
- Public PoC lowers the bar for weaponization

## Recommended Actions
- Identify arm64 hosts running KVM with untrusted/multi-tenant guests
- Apply kernel updates addressing CVE-2026-46316 as soon as available from your distro
- Prioritize patching on shared/cloud arm64 virtualization hosts
- Where patching lags, restrict who can run untrusted guest workloads on arm64 KVM
- Monitor for the public PoC / exploit attempts against vGIC-ITS

## Summary
Attackers exploited CVE-2026-5426 in the **KnowledgeDeliver LMS** to achieve unauthenticated remote code execution by leveraging hard-coded ASP.NET `machineKey` values. The attack chain deploys the **Godzilla (BLUEBEAM) web shell** and delivers a **Cobalt Strike Beacon** on vulnerable internet-facing servers.

4. Deploys Godzilla (BLUEBEAM) web shell for persistent access
5. Drops Cobalt Strike Beacon for C2

## Hunting / Detection
- Look for unusual ViewState-based POST requests on LMS endpoints
- Hunt for Godzilla/BLUEBEAM web shell IOCs in web directories

# CVE-2026-4342 — ingress-nginx Comment-Based Configuration Injection

⚠️ **Verification:** Unverified — 2 independent sources

**Date**: 2026-04-08
**Tags**: #cve #kubernetes #ingress-nginx #rce #config-injection #high
**Source**: X (@kubernetesio) — [https://x.com/kubernetesio/status/2041698295647453575](https://x.com/kubernetesio/status/2041698295647453575)
**CVE**: CVE-2026-4342
**Severity**: High
**Component**: ingress-nginx (Kubernetes ingress controller)

---

## Summary

A **high-severity** vulnerability in ingress-nginx allows an attacker to inject arbitrary nginx configuration directives via **comment-based injection** in ingress annotations or controller inputs. This can lead to nginx configuration corruption, ACL bypass, or potentially remote code execution depending on the injected directives.

---

## Affected Component

- **Project**: kubernetes/ingress-nginx
- **Version(s)**: See [GitHub issue #137893](https://github.com/kubernetes/ingress-nginx/issues/137893) for affected range
- **Attack Surface**: Kubernetes cluster with ingress-nginx installed; attacker needs ability to create/modify Ingress resources

---

## Vulnerability Details

The flaw is a **comment-based nginx configuration injection** — specially crafted input containing nginx comment syntax can break out of the expected configuration context and inject malicious directives into the final nginx.conf file generated by the ingress controller.

**Attack Scenario:**
1. Attacker has namespace-level permissions to create Ingress objects (common in multi-tenant clusters)
2. Crafts an annotation or field value containing nginx comment escape
3. Injected nginx directive lands in the generated config
4. nginx reloads, activating the injected configuration
5. Impact: bypass upstreams, redirect traffic, expose internal services, or RCE

---

## Remediation

- Apply the patch referenced in [GitHub issue #137893](https://github.com/kubernetes/ingress-nginx/issues/137893)
- Review RBAC — restrict who can create/modify Ingress resources
- Monitor ingress-nginx admission webhook logs for suspicious annotation values
- Use OPA/Kyverno policies to block unusual annotation characters

---

## References

- GitHub issue: https://github.com/kubernetes/ingress-nginx/issues/137893
- X thread: https://x.com/kubernetesio/status/2041698295647453575
- 840 likes / 166 reposts — widely flagged by the Kubernetes security community
